project.goal: Batch ETL consolidating CRO assay instrument outputs into a unified data lake
project.target_users: Data engineering team and bioinformatics consumers
project.timeline: MVP in 8 weeks with 2 CRO integrations

backend.framework: Apache Airflow 2.8 with AWS Batch operator
backend.orchestration: Airflow DAGs triggered on SFTP file arrival events

security.auth: IAM roles per CRO partition

data.db: AWS S3 data lake with Parquet format

open_questions.cro_sftp_credentials: How are CRO SFTP credentials rotated?

reasoner_reviews.product: The cost-reduction goal is clear: eliminate redundant assay data stored separately per CRO. Deduplication by compound ID and assay protocol fingerprint is the right unit of identity for this domain. Prioritize the two highest-volume CROs in MVP.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Airflow on AWS Batch is appropriate for nightly batch cadence. The dedup logic using compound ID plus assay protocol fingerprint hash is sound; recommend persisting the fingerprint alongside each record in the Glue catalog table for lineage. Unit normalization rules should be versioned in the normalization_rules entity to enable reproducibility.
reasoner_reviews.security: 21 CFR Part 11 requires an immutable audit trail for every transformation. S3 Object Lock with WORM mode on the processed zone bucket addresses immutability. IAM roles scoped per CRO source bucket prevent cross-source data leakage.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-hour nightly window is achievable for moderate data volumes. AWS Batch auto-scaling keeps costs within $800/month for ETL jobs. SFTP credential rotation must be managed via Secrets Manager to avoid hardcoded credentials in DAG configs.

backend.service_design: Airflow DAGs with separate ingestion, validation, normalization, and dedup stages as discrete tasks; each stage writes to a separate S3 prefix
backend.api_patterns: No external API; internal Airflow REST API for DAG triggering and monitoring
backend.background_jobs: Nightly scheduled DAG per CRO source; dedup comparison job runs after all CRO ingestions complete
backend.failure_handling: Task retries with exponential backoff; failed records quarantined to error/ S3 prefix for manual review
backend.backend_risks: Unit conversion rule gaps for novel assay types; CRO schema changes breaking ingestion without prior notice

security.auth_design: IAM roles assumed by Batch job containers; no long-lived credentials
security.secrets_management: Secrets Manager stores SFTP host keys and credentials; rotated every 90 days
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Transformation audit log written to append-only S3 prefix with SSE-KMS; each record carries source_cro, ingestion_timestamp, and transformation_version
security.security_risks: CRO SFTP servers may use weak ciphers; mandate minimum TLS 1.2 and key-based auth in CRO onboarding SLA

data.entities: assay_runs (id, cro_id, instrument_type, run_date, raw_s3_key), instrument_outputs (id, assay_run_id, compound_id, raw_value, raw_unit, normalized_value, normalized_unit), cro_submissions (id, cro_id, submission_date, file_count, status), compound_batches (id, compound_id, batch_number, synthesis_date), normalization_rules (id, source_unit, target_unit, conversion_factor, version, effective_date), dedup_logs (id, compound_id, protocol_fingerprint, action ENUM kept/deduplicated, processed_at)
data.storage_design: Raw zone: S3/raw/{cro_id}/{date}/; Processed zone: S3/processed/parquet/ partitioned by cro_id and run_date; Glue tables on processed zone
data.retention_and_deletion: WORM Object Lock on processed zone for 7 years; raw zone lifecycle to Glacier after 1 year
data.data_risks: Schema drift from CRO instrument software upgrades; mitigate with versioned schema registry in Glue

devops.deployment_topology: Airflow on EC2 (t3.medium) for scheduler and webserver; Batch jobs on ECS Fargate; S3 and Glue in same region
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, pytest, build Docker image, push to ECR, update Airflow DAG definitions via S3 DAG bucket sync
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch Metrics for Batch job duration and error rates; Airflow DAG run metrics exported to CloudWatch; SNS for failure notifications
devops.devops_risks: Airflow version upgrades require DAG compatibility testing; use pinned provider versions

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a 21 CFR Part 11-compliant batch ETL pipeline that consolidates CRO assay data into an AWS S3 data lake. Key decisions: Airflow DAGs on AWS Batch for serverless orchestration within the 4-hour nightly window, compound_id plus assay_protocol_fingerprint as the dedup key for domain-appropriate deduplication, versioned normalization_rules entity for reproducible unit conversions, and S3 Object Lock WORM mode for regulatory immutability. The moderate complexity is driven by multi-CRO schema variation, not data volume.
target_output.title: CRO Assay Data Lake ETL Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A batch ETL pipeline on AWS that ingests raw assay instrument outputs from multiple contract research organizations via SFTP, normalizes measurement units using versioned conversion rules, deduplicates records by compound ID and assay protocol fingerprint, and registers clean Parquet datasets in the AWS Glue Data Catalog. Designed for 21 CFR Part 11 compliance with immutable audit trails and IAM-scoped CRO data partitioning.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven Airflow DAGs trigger on SFTP file arrival via S3 EventBridge notifications, dispatching AWS Batch jobs on ECS Fargate for each ETL stage. The pipeline flows through four discrete S3 zones: raw (CRO drop files), validated (schema-checked), normalized (unit-converted), and processed (deduplicated Parquet). The Glue Data Catalog registers processed tables for Athena consumption by downstream bioinformatics tools. S3 SSE-KMS encrypts all zones. Transformation lineage is captured in append-only audit logs per 21 CFR Part 11.
target_output.technology_stack: Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.8 with AWS Batch operator. Runtime: Python 3.12 in Docker on ECS Fargate. Storage: AWS S3 with SSE-KMS, Parquet format via PyArrow. Catalog: AWS Glue Data Catalog with Athena access. Chemistry: RDKit for compound ID normalization and fingerprint generation. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with ECR image push. Monitoring: AWS CloudWatch, SNS alerts.
target_output.functional_feature_map: CRO SFTP Ingestion: file arrival detection via S3 EventBridge triggers per-CRO DAG run. Schema Validation: column presence, type checks, and assay protocol code lookup against normalization_rules. Unit Normalization: convert raw measurement units (nM, uM, % inhibition) to canonical units using versioned conversion factors stored in normalization_rules table. Deduplication Engine: compute SHA-256 fingerprint from compound_id and assay_protocol_code; reject duplicate records, log to dedup_logs. Glue Catalog Registration: update table partitions after each successful run.
target_output.system_components: Airflow Scheduler (EC2 t3.medium): DAG trigger and task coordination. AWS Batch Job Queue (ECS Fargate): stateless Python ETL worker containers per stage. S3 Raw Zone: CRO SFTP drops with per-CRO prefix. S3 Processed Zone: deduplicated Parquet tables partitioned by cro_id and run_date, WORM Object Lock enabled. AWS Glue Data Catalog: schema registry for Athena queries. Secrets Manager: SFTP credentials and KMS key references. CloudWatch: job metrics and DAG run history.
target_output.workflows: CRO Ingestion Workflow: SFTP server drops CSV/XML files to S3 raw zone triggering EventBridge event, which activates the per-CRO Airflow DAG. Stage 1 Batch job validates schema against the CRO-specific template; invalid files quarantined to error/ prefix with SNS alert. Stage 2 normalizes units using the active normalization_rules version. Stage 3 deduplication job computes protocol fingerprints, queries existing processed zone fingerprints via Glue, marks duplicates in dedup_logs. Stage 4 writes clean Parquet to processed zone and updates Glue partitions. Nightly Deduplication Cross-CRO: a reconciliation DAG runs after all per-CRO DAGs complete, comparing incoming assay records across CRO sources using compound_id to flag duplicate compound coverage across organizations, generating a cro_overlap_report stored in S3.
target_output.data_model: assay_runs (id UUID, cro_id VARCHAR, instrument_type ENUM gc_ms/hplc/plate_reader/nmr, run_date DATE, raw_s3_key TEXT, ingestion_timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). instrument_outputs (id UUID, assay_run_id FK, compound_id VARCHAR, raw_value NUMERIC, raw_unit VARCHAR, normalized_value NUMERIC, normalized_unit VARCHAR, transformation_version VARCHAR, dedup_status ENUM unique/duplicate). cro_submissions (id UUID, cro_id FK, submission_date DATE, file_count INT, status ENUM pending/processing/complete/failed). compound_batches (id UUID, compound_id VARCHAR, batch_number VARCHAR, synthesis_date DATE). normalization_rules (id UUID, source_unit VARCHAR, target_unit VARCHAR, conversion_factor NUMERIC, version VARCHAR, effective_date DATE, deprecated_at DATE). dedup_logs (id UUID, compound_id VARCHAR, protocol_fingerprint VARCHAR, action ENUM kept/deduplicated, source_cro VARCHAR, processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No external HTTP API. Airflow REST API (internal): POST /dags/{cro_dag_id}/dagRuns (trigger manual run), GET /dags/{cro_dag_id}/dagRuns/{run_id} (check status). AWS Batch API: SubmitJob for each ETL stage. Athena SQL interface for downstream consumers querying Glue-registered tables: SELECT compound_id, normalized_value, normalized_unit FROM processed.instrument_outputs WHERE cro_id = 'CRO_ALPHA' AND run_date > '2026-01-01'.
target_output.security_and_compliance: 21 CFR Part 11 controls: immutable audit trail in append-only S3 prefix with WORM Object Lock; transformation_version tracked on every output record. IAM roles scoped per CRO source bucket (cro_alpha_read, cro_beta_read) prevent cross-CRO data access. S3 SSE-KMS with customer-managed keys per data classification zone. Secrets Manager for SFTP credentials with 90-day rotation policy. VPC endpoints for S3 and Glue API calls to avoid traffic traversal over public internet. CloudTrail enabled on all S3 bucket operations for access auditing.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI pipeline: Python linting (ruff), unit tests (pytest with 80% coverage gate), Docker image build and ECR push on merge to main. Airflow DAG definitions deployed via S3 DAG bucket sync on pipeline completion. Environments: dev (single Fargate task per stage, small S3 buckets), staging (multi-CRO test fixtures, Glue catalog mirror), production (Fargate auto-scaling on Batch job queue, WORM buckets). Rollback: re-run Airflow DAG from failed task using checkpoint in S3. Infrastructure as code via Terraform managing Batch job definitions, S3 buckets with lifecycle policies, and Glue crawlers.
target_output.observability: AWS CloudWatch metrics: Batch job duration per stage, failed job count per CRO, dedup rate (duplicates/total records) per run. Airflow DAG metrics exported to CloudWatch via StatsD. CloudWatch Alarms: job failure rate >5% triggers SNS to on-call data engineer. Custom metrics: normalization_rules version lag (days since last rule update), dedup_rate spike (>20% in single run may indicate CRO data quality issue). Log groups: /aws/batch/etl-stages with structured JSON including cro_id, stage, record_count, and error_detail.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $600/month for 2 CRO integrations: Fargate tasks ~$200 (4-hour nightly window), S3 storage ~$150 (Parquet compression ~70%), Glue crawler ~$20, CloudWatch ~$30. Scale to 8 CROs within $800/month budget via Parquet compression and Spot Batch instances for non-critical stages. Glacier lifecycle after 1 year reduces storage cost for raw files by ~60%.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (6 weeks): CRO Alpha and CRO Beta CSV ingestion, schema validation, unit normalization for the 5 most common assay types, deduplication by compound_id + protocol_fingerprint, Glue catalog registration, CloudWatch monitoring. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Onboard 4 additional CROs including XML format support, cross-CRO deduplication reconciliation report, normalization_rules versioning UI for data engineers. Phase 3 (4 weeks): SFTP monitoring automation, anomaly detection on dedup rates, Athena views for bioinformatics consumer teams.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python type hints required on all ETL functions. Unit tests for normalization conversion logic covering edge cases (zero values, out-of-range units). Integration tests using sanitized CRO fixture files in CI. Normalization rule changes require a PR with before/after sample output comparison. CRO schema templates versioned in Git alongside DAG code. Airflow DAGs use TaskFlow API for clean data passing between stages.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CRO schema changes without advance notice break ingestion. Mitigation: versioned schema templates with schema drift detection alerting before failure. Risk: unit normalization rules incomplete for novel assay types from new CROs. Mitigation: quarantine unmappable records to error/ prefix rather than silently dropping. Tradeoff: WORM Object Lock on processed zone prevents in-place correction of bad records; resolve by writing corrected records as new partition versions with supersedes_run_id reference. Risk: assay protocol fingerprint collisions for similar but distinct protocols requiring manual disambiguation rules.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected SHA-256 fingerprint over MD5 for dedup keys to avoid collision risk at scale. Confirmed Parquet over ORC for broad Athena and Spark compatibility. Chose per-CRO IAM roles over a shared pipeline role to enable fine-grained audit trails per data source. Retained raw CRO files in S3 for 1 year to support re-processing if normalization rules are corrected.

metadata.notes: 21 CFR Part 11 batch ETL consolidating multi-CRO assay data with dedup and unit normalization.

project_goal.rationale: Core target identification infrastructure for clinical-stage genomics programs

target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of annotated variant data

project.goal: Annotate WES VCF files from clinical trial subjects against ClinVar, gnomAD, and internal databases
project.target_users: Computational biologists and target identification scientists
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks with 200-subject cohort capacity

backend.framework: Nextflow DSL2 with Google Cloud Batch executor
backend.annotation: bcftools, GATK annotation tools, custom Python annotators for internal DB

security.auth: Workload Identity Federation

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for pipeline_runs and qc_reports metadata
data.storage: GCS buckets per data sensitivity tier

reasoner_reviews.product: The target identification use case requires high annotation accuracy over speed. ClinVar and gnomAD are the correct starting databases. The QC flagging of high variant rate samples is essential for filtering low-quality WES runs before they contaminate downstream analysis.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Nextflow scatter-gather on Cloud Batch is the right architecture for parallelizing per-subject VCF annotation. Key improvement needed from round 1: the annotation database sync strategy. ClinVar and gnomAD release monthly updates; the pipeline must track annotation database versions in pipeline_runs metadata so results are reproducible when databases are updated. Redis caching of inter-process state needs explicit TTL configuration to prevent stale job state accumulation.
reasoner_reviews.security: GDPR Article 9 for genetic data requires explicit consent tracking per subject. The pseudonymization approach for EU subjects must be documented with the pseudonymization key stored separately from annotated data. GCS CMEK with separate keys per trial is required. VPC Service Controls must prevent data exfiltration to external GCS buckets.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 24-hour SLA for 200 subjects at 100K variants each requires parallelism of at least 50 concurrent Cloud Batch jobs. Preemptible VM use should be limited to annotation stages, not VCF upload or QC report stages where interruption causes data loss.

backend.service_design: Nextflow DSL2 pipeline with distinct process blocks: vcf_ingest, quality_filter, clinvar_annotate, gnomad_annotate, internal_db_annotate, variant_aggregation, qc_report_generate
backend.background_jobs: Nightly annotation database sync job pulls latest ClinVar and gnomAD VCF releases to versioned GCS prefixes; pipeline runs reference a pinned database version
backend.failure_handling: Nextflow resume capability for interrupted runs using checkpointing; failed samples quarantined with error_code in pipeline_runs table
backend.backend_risks: Annotation database version drift between runs makes cross-cohort comparisons invalid; must version-pin per pipeline_run

security.auth_design: Workload Identity per Nextflow process container; no service account keys
security.authorization_model: GCS bucket-level IAM: trial-vcf-bucket (ingest role), annotation-db-bucket (read-only), results-bucket (write for pipeline, read for researchers)
security.privacy_controls: EU subject pseudonymization: subject_id replaced with pseudonym_id derived from HMAC-SHA256; pseudonymization key stored in Secret Manager separate from data
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Cloud Audit Logs on all GCS operations; pipeline_runs table captures who triggered run, annotation versions, and subject count
security.security_risks: Re-identification risk if annotated variants combined with public genomic databases; output tables must not include directly identifying trial subject metadata

data.entities: vcf_files (id UUID, subject_id FK, trial_arm VARCHAR, wes_capture_kit VARCHAR, sequencing_date DATE, gcs_path TEXT, file_size_gb NUMERIC, quality_pass BOOLEAN). variants (id UUID, vcf_file_id FK, chromosome VARCHAR, position BIGINT, ref_allele TEXT, alt_allele TEXT, variant_type ENUM snv/indel/cnv). gene_annotations (id UUID, variant_id FK, gene_symbol VARCHAR, consequence VARCHAR, clinvar_significance VARCHAR, gnomad_af NUMERIC, internal_db_af NUMERIC, annotation_db_version VARCHAR). clinical_trial_subjects (id UUID, pseudonym_id VARCHAR, trial_arm VARCHAR, enrollment_date DATE, jurisdiction ENUM us/eu). pipeline_runs (id UUID, triggered_by VARCHAR, run_date TIMESTAMPTZ, subject_count INT, annotation_db_versions JSONB, status ENUM running/complete/failed). qc_reports (id UUID, pipeline_run_id FK, subject_id FK, total_variants INT, ts_tv_ratio NUMERIC, high_variant_flag BOOLEAN, flag_reason TEXT).
data.storage_design: GCS tiered: vcf-raw/ (ingestion, CMEK key A), annotation-db/ (shared read, CMEK key B), results/ (annotated outputs, CMEK key A, VPC-SC enforced)
data.analytics_events: Variant frequency tables in BigQuery for cross-cohort queries; published after QC pass

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Build triggers on Git tag; builds annotator Docker images, pushes to Artifact Registry, updates Nextflow pipeline config with new image digests
devops.environments: dev (10-subject fixture cohort), staging (50-subject anonymized cohort), production (full trial cohorts, europe-west1 for EU data)
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus pushgateway for Nextflow metrics; Grafana dashboards: pipeline duration per stage, failed sample count, annotation DB cache hit rate; Alertmanager for duration SLA breach
devops.rollback_strategy: Nextflow pipeline versioned via Git tag; re-run prior version using pinned container images and database version references in pipeline_runs

ISS-001.title: Annotation database version not tracked per pipeline run
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan wrote annotated variants to results/ but did not capture which ClinVar and gnomAD release versions were used. Cross-cohort comparisons become invalid when databases update between runs.

ISS-002.title: EU subject pseudonymization key colocation risk
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan stored pseudonymization keys in the same Secret Manager project as pipeline service accounts, creating single-compromise risk for re-identification.

history.note: Raised by security review

focus_issues.title: EU subject pseudonymization key colocation risk
focus_issues.detail: Pseudonymization keys must be in a separate GCP project from pipeline service accounts.

Move pseudonymization key storage to a dedicated Secret Manager project isolated from pipeline service accounts

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity issues unresolved affecting data reproducibility and GDPR compliance

previous_audits.summary: Strong pipeline architecture and Nextflow selection are appropriate, but critical gaps in annotation database versioning and GDPR pseudonymization key management prevent approval.
strengths: Nextflow scatter-gather correctly parallelizes per-subject annotation
GCS tiered storage with CMEK is appropriate for genomic PHI
QC flagging logic for high-variant-rate samples is domain-appropriate
concerns: No annotation database version captured in pipeline_runs metadata
Pseudonymization keys stored in same project as pipeline compute
blocking_issues: Annotation DB version gap makes cross-cohort comparisons unreproducible
GDPR pseudonymization key isolation is required for Article 9 compliance
recommendations: Add annotation_db_versions JSONB to pipeline_runs capturing ClinVar build and gnomAD release per run
Isolate pseudonymization key Secret Manager to a separate GCP project with break-glass access policy

issue_updates.title: Annotation database version not tracked
issue_updates.detail: pipeline_runs table lacks annotation DB version fields

issue_updates.title: Pseudonymization key colocation
issue_updates.detail: Keys stored in same project as compute service accounts

previous_plan.title: Genomic Variant Annotation Pipeline v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Nextflow DSL2 pipeline on Google Cloud Batch annotating WES VCF files from clinical trial subjects against ClinVar and gnomAD, with QC reporting and variant frequency aggregation for target identification.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Nextflow scatter-gather on Cloud Batch distributing per-subject VCF annotation jobs. GCS tiered storage with CMEK. PostgreSQL 16 for pipeline run metadata.
previous_plan.data_model: vcf_files, variants, gene_annotations, pipeline_runs, qc_reports - missing annotation_db_versions tracking.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: GCS CMEK, Workload Identity, GDPR pseudonymization - pseudonymization keys stored in same Secret Manager project as pipeline accounts (identified gap).

target_output.thinking_summary: Revising the genomic variant annotation pipeline to address two high-severity issues from round 1. ISS-001 resolved by adding annotation_db_versions JSONB to pipeline_runs and implementing a versioned annotation database sync job that pins each pipeline execution to a specific ClinVar build and gnomAD release. ISS-002 resolved by isolating GDPR pseudonymization keys in a separate GCP project (genomics-pseudonym-vault) with break-glass IAM conditions, decoupled from pipeline service accounts. Both changes are critical for GDPR Article 9 compliance and cross-cohort reproducibility in IND-supporting analytics.

fix_report.action_taken: Added annotation_db_versions JSONB field to pipeline_runs table capturing ClinVar build ID and gnomAD release tag. Nightly annotation DB sync job now writes a version manifest to GCS. Nextflow pipeline reads the latest manifest at run start and pins all annotator processes to that version, recording the pinned versions in pipeline_runs on completion.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Every pipeline_run record will carry the exact annotation database versions used, enabling reproducible cross-cohort comparisons and audit trail for IND submissions.

fix_report.action_taken: Created a dedicated GCP project (genomics-pseudonym-vault) to host pseudonymization key Secret Manager instance. Pipeline service accounts in the main project have no IAM access to genomics-pseudonym-vault. Pseudonymization is performed in an isolated Cloud Run Job that fetches the key, transforms subject IDs, and returns only pseudonym_ids without exposing the key to pipeline containers.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Compromise of pipeline service accounts cannot expose GDPR pseudonymization keys, meeting Article 9 data minimization and separation requirements.

target_output.title: Genomic Variant Annotation Pipeline v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Nextflow DSL2 pipeline on Google Cloud Batch that annotates whole-exome sequencing VCF files from clinical trial subjects against version-pinned ClinVar, gnomAD, and internal proprietary databases. Parallelizes annotation across hundreds of subjects using scatter-gather, generates per-sample QC reports, and produces variant frequency tables in BigQuery for target identification analysis. HIPAA and GDPR Article 9 compliant with isolated pseudonymization key management and 24-hour SLA for 200-subject cohorts.
target_output.architecture_overview: Nextflow DSL2 scatter-gather pipeline executes on Google Cloud Batch preemptible VMs (n2-standard-8) with per-subject parallelism. A versioned annotation database sync job runs nightly, writing ClinVar and gnomAD release manifests to GCS. Each pipeline run reads the latest version manifest and pins annotation processes to specific database snapshot paths in GCS. EU subject pseudonymization is performed in an isolated Cloud Run Job in a separate GCP project (genomics-pseudonym-vault) before VCF ingestion, ensuring subject IDs never enter the main pipeline project. Results are written to GCS results/ with VPC Service Controls and published to BigQuery for researcher queries.
target_output.technology_stack: Workflow: Nextflow DSL2 with Google Cloud Batch executor on n2-standard-8 preemptible VMs. Annotation tools: bcftools annotate, custom Python 3.12 annotators for internal DB. Databases: ClinVar VCF (monthly release), gnomAD r4.0 (versioned GCS snapshot), internal variant DB (PostgreSQL 16). Metadata: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL. State: Redis 7 on Cloud Memorystore. Storage: GCS CMEK with VPC Service Controls. CI/CD: Cloud Build. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana, Cloud Logging.
target_output.functional_feature_map: VCF Ingestion: validated upload to vcf-raw/ GCS prefix with checksum verification. Subject Pseudonymization: isolated Cloud Run Job in genomics-pseudonym-vault project replaces subject_id with HMAC-SHA256 pseudonym_id. Annotation: scatter per subject across ClinVar, gnomAD, and internal DB processes in parallel, each pinned to versioned database snapshot. QC Reporting: ts/tv ratio calculation, coverage depth check, high variant rate flagging (>99th percentile of cohort). Variant Aggregation: frequency statistics per trial arm written to BigQuery. Pipeline Metadata: pipeline_runs record with annotation_db_versions JSONB.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Batch Job Queue: preemptible n2-standard-8 VMs executing Nextflow processes. GCS vcf-raw/ bucket: CMEK key A, VPC Service Controls. GCS annotation-db/ bucket: versioned ClinVar and gnomAD snapshots, version manifests. GCS results/ bucket: annotated output VCFs and QC report JSONs. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: pipeline_runs, vcf_files, qc_reports, clinical_trial_subjects metadata. Cloud Memorystore Redis 7: Nextflow process state and deduplication caches. genomics-pseudonym-vault GCP project: isolated Secret Manager for HMAC keys, Cloud Run Job for pseudonymization. BigQuery: variant_frequencies and qc_summary datasets for researcher access.
target_output.workflows: VCF Annotation Workflow: computational biologist uploads subject VCFs to staging GCS prefix and triggers pipeline run via Cloud Build webhook. Pseudonymization Cloud Run Job in genomics-pseudonym-vault reads subject manifest, replaces subject_ids with pseudonym_ids, and returns mapping (stored only in pseudonym-vault project). Nextflow pipeline reads latest version manifest (e.g., ClinVar_20260301, gnomAD_r4.0.1) and scatters 200 parallel Cloud Batch jobs annotating each pseudonymized VCF. Jobs write annotated VCFs to results/. Aggregation process collects per-subject variant files and computes trial-arm frequency statistics, publishing to BigQuery. QC Report Generation: after annotation, a Nextflow QC process computes ts/tv ratio, mean coverage depth, and variant count per subject; samples exceeding the 99th percentile variant rate are flagged in qc_reports with flag_reason='high_variant_rate'; pipeline coordinator is notified via Cloud Pub/Sub alert for manual review before results are promoted to the analysis tier.
target_output.data_model: vcf_files (id UUID, pseudonym_id VARCHAR, trial_arm VARCHAR, wes_capture_kit VARCHAR, sequencing_date DATE, gcs_path TEXT, file_size_gb NUMERIC, quality_pass BOOLEAN). variants (id UUID, vcf_file_id FK, chromosome VARCHAR, position BIGINT, ref_allele TEXT, alt_allele TEXT, variant_type ENUM snv/indel/cnv). gene_annotations (id UUID, variant_id FK, gene_symbol VARCHAR, consequence VARCHAR, clinvar_significance VARCHAR, gnomad_af NUMERIC, internal_db_af NUMERIC, annotation_db_version VARCHAR). clinical_trial_subjects (id UUID, pseudonym_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, trial_arm VARCHAR, enrollment_date DATE, jurisdiction ENUM us/eu) - no direct identifiers in main project. pipeline_runs (id UUID, triggered_by VARCHAR, run_date TIMESTAMPTZ, subject_count INT, annotation_db_versions JSONB e.g. {clinvar: ClinVar_20260301, gnomad: r4.0.1, internal_db: v2.4}, status ENUM running/complete/failed). qc_reports (id UUID, pipeline_run_id FK, pseudonym_id FK, total_variants INT, ts_tv_ratio NUMERIC, high_variant_flag BOOLEAN, flag_reason TEXT).
target_output.api_design: No external HTTP API. Cloud Build webhook: POST /trigger/pipeline-run with payload {trial_arm, vcf_manifest_gcs_path, annotation_manifest_version}. BigQuery SQL interface for researchers: SELECT gene_symbol, COUNT(*) as carrier_count FROM variant_frequencies.gene_annotations WHERE trial_arm = 'ARM_A' AND clinvar_significance = 'Pathogenic' GROUP BY gene_symbol ORDER BY carrier_count DESC. Cloud Logging structured query for pipeline ops: resource.type=cloud_batch_job AND jsonPayload.pipeline_run_id={run_id}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HIPAA controls: GCS CMEK with separate keys per trial (key A for subject data, key B for annotation databases), VPC Service Controls perimeter blocking exfiltration to external GCS buckets, Cloud Audit Logs on all GCS and BigQuery operations. GDPR Article 9: pseudonymization in isolated genomics-pseudonym-vault GCP project; HMAC-SHA256 keys stored in that project's Secret Manager with break-glass IAM condition (MFA + justification required for human access); EU subject data processed only in europe-west1 region. ICH E6 GCP: annotation_db_versions captured in pipeline_runs for data lineage; pipeline versioned by Git tag with container image digests pinned for reproducibility.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Build CI: on Git tag push, builds Nextflow annotator Docker images, pushes to Artifact Registry with image digest, updates Nextflow config nextflow.config with pinned digests. Environments: dev (europe-west1, 10-subject fixture VCFs, Cloud SQL dev instance), staging (anonymized 50-subject cohort), production (Cloud SQL HA, VPC Service Controls enforced, Cloud Armor on ingress). Nightly annotation DB sync: Cloud Scheduler triggers Cloud Run Job that downloads latest ClinVar and gnomAD VCF releases, validates checksums, writes to versioned GCS prefix, and updates version manifest. Rollback: re-run pipeline with prior Git tag and prior annotation manifest version using pipeline_runs record as reference.
target_output.observability: Prometheus pushgateway collects Nextflow process metrics exported by a sidecar container. Grafana dashboards: pipeline_run duration (target <24h for 200 subjects), per-stage median duration, annotation DB cache hit rate (Redis), failed subject count per run, high-variant-flag rate per cohort. Alertmanager rules: pipeline duration >20h triggers PagerDuty (approaching SLA breach), failed_subject_count >5 triggers Slack alert to computational biology team. Cloud Logging: structured JSON logs per Nextflow process including pseudonym_id (not subject_id), pipeline_run_id, stage_name, duration_seconds, annotation_db_version.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $1400/month for 200-subject monthly cohort runs: Cloud Batch preemptible VMs ~$800 (200 concurrent jobs x 2h average annotation), Cloud SQL ~$200, GCS storage ~$300 (compressed VCFs and Parquet annotation outputs). Scale to 500-subject cohorts by increasing Cloud Batch quota and partitioning annotation scatter into 2 waves. Preemptible VMs provide 60-80% cost reduction vs on-demand for annotation stages.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): VCF ingestion pipeline, ClinVar and gnomAD annotation, pseudonymization in genomics-pseudonym-vault, QC report generation for 200-subject cohort, annotation_db_versions tracking in pipeline_runs, BigQuery variant frequency tables. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Internal proprietary variant database integration, somatic variant calling module, enhanced QC metrics (allele balance, off-target rate). Phase 3 (8 weeks): Cross-trial cohort meta-analysis pipeline, automated annotation database update CI, variant interpretation API for target validation scientists.
target_output.development_guidelines: Nextflow DSL2 modules versioned independently with semantic versioning. Container images built from pinned base images in Artifact Registry. Annotation database versions referenced by content hash, not mutable path. All PHI handling code reviewed by security team before merge. Integration tests use synthetic VCF fixtures generated from GRCh38 reference without real subject data. Pipeline reruns must be idempotent: re-annotating the same VCF with the same annotation manifest version must produce identical outputs.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: gnomAD release frequency changes break annotation pipeline compatibility. Mitigation: annotation database adapter abstraction with versioned compatibility matrix tested in CI. Risk: Cloud Batch preemptible VM interruption during VCF annotation causes partial outputs. Mitigation: Nextflow checkpointing and per-subject output atomicity (write to temp prefix, rename on success). Tradeoff: pseudonymization in a separate GCP project adds latency (~5 minutes per cohort) but is required for GDPR Article 9 key isolation. Risk: ts/tv ratio QC threshold too sensitive for targeted WES panels vs whole-exome, causing high false-positive QC flags; tune thresholds per capture kit type in qc_reports.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected HMAC-SHA256 over AES encryption for pseudonymization to enable consistency checks without decryption. Confirmed annotation_db_versions as JSONB to allow additional databases without schema migration. Chose Cloud Batch over Dataflow for Nextflow native compatibility. EU data residency constraint met by europe-west1 region with VPC Service Controls blocking cross-region data movement.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision fixing annotation DB versioning and GDPR pseudonymization key isolation for high-scale clinical genomics pipeline.

project_goal.value: Replace SOAP-based ELN integration layer with a RESTful API exposing compound registration, protocol retrieval, and experiment result submission to internal informatics tooling
project_goal.rationale: Legacy SOAP layer is brittle and blocks modern tooling adoption

target_users.value: Internal informatics teams, chemistry design tooling, and ELN client applications
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in stakeholder workshops

project_class.rationale: No UI required; pure REST API consumed by internal tooling

capabilities.rationale: API-first with auth and structured data persistence

complexity_level.rationale: SOAP-to-REST migration with backward-compatibility shaping and 21 CFR Part 11 audit requirements

risk_level.rationale: Confidential compound data but internal exposure only; SOAP migration introduces transient dual-mode risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: Compound structures, protocols, and experiment results are proprietary IP

external_exposure.rationale: Consumed exclusively by internal informatics systems behind corporate network

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials via Azure AD OIDC; per-client scopes for read vs write operations
access_model.rationale: Service-to-service auth without user sessions; scoped to limit blast radius

feature_scope.value: Compound registration with SMILES validation, protocol fetch with backward-compatible XML shaping, experiment result submission, audit log retrieval
feature_scope.rationale: Core ELN integration surfaces identified from SOAP WSDL analysis

mvp_scope.value: Compound registration and protocol retrieval endpoints with 21 CFR Part 11 audit logging and Azure AD auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum for chemistry team migration off SOAP layer

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest, Azure AD client credentials, audit log immutability, input validation on all endpoints
security_baseline.rationale: Corporate security baseline plus 21 CFR Part 11 tamper-evidence requirements

frontend_stack.rationale: Pure API service; no UI component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin framework
backend_stack.rationale: Low latency and strong concurrency for high-throughput informatics calls; single binary deployment on AKS

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for compound registry and audit log; Redis 7 for response caching of stable protocol records
data_platform.rationale: Relational integrity for compound master data; Redis reduces repeated protocol fetch load

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service with Horizontal Pod Autoscaler
hosting_target.rationale: Corporate AKS standard; supports rolling deployments and canary for migration safety

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audit logs retained 5 years per 21 CFR Part 11; compound records retained indefinitely; Redis TTL 1 hour for cached protocol responses
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Regulatory retention requirement for electronic records in regulated workflows

future_scope.value: External partner API exposure with mTLS, GraphQL gateway for complex compound queries, webhook push for experiment completions
future_scope.rationale: Post-migration roadmap items deferred from current scope

constraints.value: Must maintain backward-compatible XML response shaping for legacy ELN clients during 6-month migration window; AKS cluster is shared with other services
constraints.rationale: ELN clients cannot all migrate simultaneously; shared cluster requires resource quotas

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM traces on all endpoints, structured JSON logs, custom metrics for compound registration rate and SOAP-compat request volume
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate Datadog standard; SOAP-compat metric tracks migration progress

execution_preference.value: Incremental endpoint delivery with parallel SOAP layer kept live until each endpoint reaches 100% client migration
execution_preference.rationale: Zero-disruption cutover strategy for internal clients

compliance_context.value: 21 CFR Part 11 electronic records and signatures; SOC 2 Type II for audit log integrity and access controls
compliance_context.rationale: Regulated lab workflows require electronic record tamper-evidence and audit trail

project.goal: Replace SOAP ELN integration layer with REST API
project.target_users: Internal informatics tooling and ELN clients

backend.api: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1 and XML shaping adapter for legacy clients

security.auth: Azure AD OIDC client credentials flow
security.audit: Immutable audit_log table with tamper-evident SHA-256 chain hashing

constraints.backward_compat: XML response shaping active for 6-month window

reasoner_reviews.product: The SOAP-to-REST migration strategy with a 6-month parallel run is the correct approach for ELN clients that cannot migrate simultaneously. Compound registration and protocol retrieval are the highest-priority endpoints to unblock the chemistry team.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Gin is a strong choice for this API-first service given the high call frequency from informatics tooling. The XML shaping adapter should be implemented as a middleware layer rather than duplicated per-handler to keep the codebase maintainable during the migration window. Redis caching for protocol records will absorb repetitive fetches that were previously cached at the SOAP layer.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure AD client credentials with per-client scopes is the right model for service-to-service auth. 21 CFR Part 11 requires audit log entries to be tamper-evident; recommend SHA-256 chaining of sequential audit records with genesis anchor in Azure Key Vault. Secrets for DB credentials must rotate through Key Vault references in AKS.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Shared AKS cluster requires explicit resource quotas and PodDisruptionBudgets to prevent noisy-neighbor impact during peak informatics batch runs. The 6-month XML compatibility window must have a clear deprecation metric so the migration has a defined end state.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 3 plan resolved the Redis TTL misconfiguration but the audit log hash-chaining mechanism lacked anchor specification. Round 4 must close ISS-001 by specifying genesis anchor storage in Azure Key Vault with RFC 3161 timestamp attestation.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with Gin router organized into domain packages: compounds (registration, SMILES validation), protocols (fetch, XML adapter), experiments (result submission), audit (append-only writes). Middleware stack: Azure AD JWT validation, request ID injection, structured logging, XML/JSON response negotiation.
backend.api_patterns: OpenAPI 3.1 spec-first. RESTful endpoints return JSON by default; Accept: application/xml header triggers XML shaping adapter for legacy ELN clients. Idempotency keys required on compound registration POST to handle retry storms from ELN tooling.
backend.business_modules: CompoundRegistration: parse SMILES via RDKit microservice, assign internal CHM-YYYYNNNNN registry ID, persist to compounds table, emit audit event. ProtocolFetch: query protocols table, check Redis cache TTL 1h, shape response per Accept header. ExperimentResultSubmission: validate result schema against protocol_definition JSONB, persist to experiment_results.
backend.failure_handling: Circuit breaker on RDKit SMILES validation service; fallback rejects registration with 503 and descriptive error. Idempotency cache in Redis prevents duplicate compound entries on retry. DB connection pool max 30 conns with queue timeout 2s.

security.auth_design: Azure AD OIDC client credentials flow; each API client has a dedicated service principal with scoped claims (compounds:write, protocols:read, experiments:write, audit:read). JWT validated at Gin middleware using JWKS endpoint with 1-hour key rotation cache.
security.authorization_model: Scope-based: compounds:write for POST /compounds, protocols:read for GET /protocols/:id, experiments:write for POST /experiments/results. audit:read scope restricted to compliance team clients only.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only audit_log table (id BIGSERIAL, event_type, resource_type, resource_id, client_id, timestamp, payload_hash, chain_hash). chain_hash = SHA-256(previous chain_hash || payload_hash). Genesis anchor (row 1 chain_hash) stored in Azure Key Vault secret with RFC 3161 timestamp attestation. Quarterly AKS CronJob verifies full chain integrity.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault with AKS CSI driver. PostgreSQL credentials rotated every 30 days. Redis auth token rotated every 90 days. No secrets in environment variables or container images.

data.entities: compounds (id UUID PK, registry_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, smiles TEXT, inchi_key VARCHAR, molecular_weight NUMERIC, status ENUM active/deprecated, created_by, created_at). protocols (id UUID PK, name, version INT, protocol_definition JSONB, deprecated_at). experiment_results (id UUID PK, compound_id FK, protocol_id FK, result_values JSONB, qc_status ENUM pending/passed/failed, submitted_by, submitted_at). audit_log (id BIGSERIAL PK, event_type, resource_type, resource_id, client_id, timestamp, payload_hash CHAR(64), chain_hash CHAR(64)).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database Flexible Server with zone-redundant HA. Read replica for audit log compliance queries. Redis 7 on Azure Cache for Redis for protocol response caching TTL 1h.
data.retention_and_deletion: audit_log retained 5 years per 21 CFR Part 11 with no UPDATE/DELETE grants. Compound records retained indefinitely; deprecated flag used instead of hard delete. Protocol records archived to cold Azure Blob Storage after 3 years inactive.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS namespace eln-api. Deployment: 3 replicas min, HPA on CPU 60% and RPS metrics up to 10 replicas. PodDisruptionBudget minAvailable 2. Resource requests 250m CPU / 256Mi RAM per pod.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: golangci-lint, go test ./... with testcontainers-go ephemeral PostgreSQL, Docker build, push ACR, Helm upgrade --atomic with auto-rollback on failed readiness probe. Canary deployment for compound registration endpoint during initial rollout.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with Go tracer. Custom metrics: compound_registrations_total (counter), protocol_cache_hit_ratio (gauge), soap_compat_requests_total (counter tracking migration progress), audit_chain_verification_result (gauge: 1=pass 0=break emitted by quarterly CronJob).
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous release on health check failure within 5 minutes of deploy. golang-migrate down migrations tested in CI before every release.

ISS-001.title: Audit log chain-hash anchor strategy unspecified
ISS-001.detail: Previous rounds described SHA-256 chaining without specifying where the genesis anchor is stored or how it is attested, leaving the tamper-evidence claim unverifiable for 21 CFR Part 11 auditors.

ISS-002.title: Redis TTL for protocol cache not defined
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 plan omitted TTL configuration for Redis protocol response cache, risking stale protocol definitions after version updates.

ISS-003.title: No deprecation metric for SOAP compatibility window
ISS-003.detail: The 6-month XML compat window had no observable metric to signal when it was safe to remove the adapter.

focus_issues.title: Audit log chain-hash anchor strategy unspecified
focus_issues.detail: Previous rounds described SHA-256 chaining without specifying genesis anchor storage or attestation.

latest_recommendations: Specify genesis anchor storage location and attestation mechanism for the audit log SHA-256 chain
Add soap_compat_requests_total metric to track migration completion

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 focus is resolving ISS-001: the audit log chain-hash anchor strategy. The fix is explicit: genesis anchor stored in Azure Key Vault with RFC 3161 timestamp attestation, and quarterly chain integrity verified by an AKS CronJob. All prior issues resolved. Go/Gin on AKS with scoped Azure AD client credentials and PostgreSQL 16 remains sound for this internal informatics API with 21 CFR Part 11 requirements.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified genesis anchor hash stored in Azure Key Vault secret 'audit-genesis-anchor' tagged with RFC 3161 timestamp attestation certificate; quarterly AKS CronJob 'audit-chain-verify' replays full chain and emits Datadog metric audit_chain_verification_result=0 on any broken link triggering P1 alert
fix_report.expected_outcome: 21 CFR Part 11 tamper-evidence requirement is addressable by audit inspectors with a verifiable, automated chain integrity mechanism

target_output.title: ELN REST API Migration Architecture — Final
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 Gin REST API deployed on Azure Kubernetes Service that replaces the legacy SOAP-based electronic lab notebook integration layer. Exposes compound registration with RDKit SMILES validation, protocol retrieval with backward-compatible XML shaping for legacy ELN clients during the 6-month migration window, and experiment result submission with protocol schema validation. Azure AD OIDC client credentials with per-scope authorization gates all access. PostgreSQL 16 stores the compound registry and an immutable 21 CFR Part 11 audit log with SHA-256 chain hashing anchored by an RFC 3161-attested genesis record in Azure Key Vault. Redis 7 caches stable protocol responses. Datadog tracks soap_compat_requests_total as the migration completion signal.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-binary Go 1.22 Gin service deployed as a 3-10 pod HPA-managed Deployment in the eln-api AKS namespace. Internal informatics tooling calls REST endpoints over TLS 1.3 on the corporate network. Azure AD OIDC JWT middleware validates client credentials and enforces per-scope authorization before routing to domain handlers. Compound registration calls an internal RDKit microservice for SMILES validation before persisting to PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server with zone-redundant HA. Protocol fetch handlers check Redis 7 (TTL 1h) before querying PostgreSQL, and negotiate JSON vs XML via Accept header using a shaping adapter that preserves legacy SOAP element names. All write operations append to the immutable audit_log with SHA-256 chain hashing; genesis anchor stored in Azure Key Vault with RFC 3161 attestation. Datadog APM traces every request; soap_compat_requests_total counter drives migration cutover decisions.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, golang-migrate, Datadog Go tracer, JWKS-validated Azure AD JWT middleware, testcontainers-go. Data: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Flexible Server (zone-redundant HA), Redis 7 on Azure Cache for Redis. Infrastructure: Azure Kubernetes Service, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault with AKS CSI driver, Azure DevOps Pipelines. Observability: Datadog APM, structured JSON logs with correlation IDs, custom Datadog metrics for migration and audit chain health.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Compound Registration: accept SMILES string, call RDKit validation microservice, assign CHM-YYYYNNNNN registry ID, persist to compounds table, emit audit event with chain hash. Protocol Retrieval: serve JSON or legacy XML per Accept header, check Redis cache TTL 1h, invalidate on protocol version bump. Experiment Result Submission: validate result payload against protocol_definition JSONB schema, persist to experiment_results. Audit Log Retrieval: paginated read-only endpoint restricted to audit:read scope. SOAP Compat Tracking: soap_compat_requests_total metric incremented per XML-negotiated response.
target_output.system_components: AKS Deployment (3-10 pods, HPA on CPU 60% and RPS). Go Gin API server with middleware stack: OIDC JWT validation, request ID, structured logging, Accept-header XML negotiation. RDKit SMILES validation microservice (separate AKS Deployment with circuit breaker). PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server (zone-redundant, read replica for audit queries). Redis 7 Azure Cache (protocol response cache TTL 1h). Azure Key Vault with CSI driver (DB credentials rotation, genesis anchor secret with RFC 3161 cert). Azure Container Registry. Datadog Agent DaemonSet. AKS CronJob audit-chain-verify (quarterly).
target_output.workflows: Compound Registration: POST /v1/compounds received with SMILES and metadata. JWT middleware validates compounds:write scope. CompoundRegistration handler calls RDKit microservice GET /validate?smiles=...; on invalid SMILES returns 422. On valid, generates registry_id CHM-YYYYNNNNN, writes to compounds table in transaction with audit_log append (chain_hash = SHA-256(prev_chain_hash || payload_hash)). Returns 201 with registry_id and id. Protocol Retrieval with XML Compat: GET /v1/protocols/:id from legacy ELN client with Accept: application/xml. JWT middleware validates protocols:read scope. Redis check: if hit returns cached XML with Content-Type: application/xml. On cache miss, queries PostgreSQL protocols table, marshals response via XML shaping adapter preserving legacy SOAP element names (e.g., ProtocolDefinitionResponse wrapper), stores in Redis TTL 1h, returns 200.
target_output.data_model: compounds (id UUID PK, registry_id VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL, smiles TEXT NOT NULL, inchi_key VARCHAR(27) UNIQUE, molecular_weight NUMERIC(10,4), status ENUM('active','deprecated') DEFAULT 'active', created_by VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). protocols (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR NOT NULL, version INTEGER NOT NULL, protocol_definition JSONB NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, deprecated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). experiment_results (id UUID PK, compound_id UUID FK->compounds, protocol_id UUID FK->protocols, result_values JSONB NOT NULL, qc_status ENUM('pending','passed','failed'), submitted_by VARCHAR, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_log (id BIGSERIAL PK, event_type VARCHAR, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id VARCHAR, client_id VARCHAR, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, payload_hash CHAR(64) NOT NULL, chain_hash CHAR(64) NOT NULL). Genesis anchor: chain_hash of row 1 stored in Azure Key Vault secret 'audit-genesis-anchor' with RFC 3161 timestamp attestation certificate.
target_output.api_design: All endpoints under /api/v1. Auth: Bearer JWT (Azure AD OIDC) validated by Gin middleware. POST /v1/compounds (scope: compounds:write, X-Idempotency-Key required): register compound, returns 201 {registry_id, id}. GET /v1/compounds/:id (scope: compounds:read): retrieve compound. GET /v1/protocols/:id (scope: protocols:read, Accept: application/json or application/xml): retrieve protocol with format negotiation. POST /v1/experiments/results (scope: experiments:write, X-Idempotency-Key required): submit experiment results. GET /v1/audit-log?resource_type=compound&from=&to= (scope: audit:read): paginated audit retrieval max 500 rows. Rate limit: 500 req/min per client_id via Redis token bucket.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD OIDC client credentials: each informatics client has a dedicated service principal with minimum-privilege scopes. JWT validated using JWKS endpoint (cached 1h, force-refresh on 401). TLS 1.3 enforced at AKS ingress. Audit log: append-only PostgreSQL table with REVOKE UPDATE, DELETE ON audit_log FROM api_role. Chain hashing: chain_hash = SHA-256(previous chain_hash || payload_hash) for each row. Genesis anchor (row 1 chain_hash) stored in Azure Key Vault secret 'audit-genesis-anchor' with RFC 3161 timestamp attestation certificate issued by trusted TSA. Quarterly AKS CronJob 'audit-chain-verify' replays full chain from genesis and emits Datadog metric audit_chain_verification_result (1=pass, 0=break); break triggers P1 PagerDuty alert. REVOKE DELETE on audit_log enforced at DB role level. 21 CFR Part 11 compliant. SOC 2 Type II: Key Vault access logs and AKS audit logs forwarded to Datadog.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS namespace eln-api. Deployment: minReplicas 3, maxReplicas 10, HPA on CPU 60% and custom RPS metric from Datadog external metrics adapter. PodDisruptionBudget minAvailable 2. Resource requests 250m CPU / 256Mi RAM, limits 1 CPU / 512Mi RAM. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines — golangci-lint (errcheck, staticcheck, gosec), go test ./... with testcontainers-go ephemeral PostgreSQL (85% coverage gate), Docker build, push to ACR, Helm upgrade --atomic (auto-rollback on readiness probe failure within 5 min). Environments: dev (1 replica, shared DB schema), staging (3 replicas, dedicated Flexible Server), production (3-10 replicas, zone-redundant Flexible Server, read replica). golang-migrate applied via Kubernetes Job pre-upgrade hook. Secrets via Azure Key Vault CSI driver; no plaintext secrets in Helm values or container images.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM Go tracer on all Gin handlers with trace IDs in structured JSON logs. Custom metrics: compound_registrations_total (counter, labels: status=success/failed), protocol_cache_hit_ratio (gauge), soap_compat_requests_total (counter — migration progress signal; decommission SOAP layer when zero for 2 consecutive weeks), audit_chain_verification_result (gauge: 1=pass 0=break, emitted quarterly). PagerDuty alerts: error_rate >1% -> P2, p99 latency >500ms -> P3, audit_chain_break_detected -> P1 immediate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS: 3 baseline pods ~$120/month; HPA to 10 pods under peak informatics batch load. Azure Flexible Server (General Purpose 4 vCores) ~$280/month with zone-redundant standby. Redis Cache C1 ~$55/month. Azure Key Vault ~$5/month. Total ~$460/month. Ceiling: 10 Gin pods handle ~5,000 req/min at 50ms p99, sufficient for projected informatics tooling call volume.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (weeks 1-4): AKS namespace and resource quotas, PostgreSQL schema via golang-migrate, Redis, Azure AD service principals with scoped claims, Key Vault CSI setup. POST /v1/compounds with SMILES validation circuit breaker and audit log with SHA-256 chain hashing; genesis anchor stored in Key Vault with RFC 3161 attestation. Phase 2 (weeks 5-8): GET /v1/protocols/:id with XML shaping adapter and Redis cache; soap_compat_requests_total metric live. Legacy ELN clients pointed at REST endpoint; SOAP layer running in parallel. Phase 3 (weeks 9-16): POST /v1/experiments/results with protocol schema validation, GET /v1/audit-log endpoint. Monitor soap_compat_requests_total decline; decommission SOAP layer when metric reaches zero for 2 consecutive weeks. AKS CronJob audit-chain-verify deployed for quarterly 21 CFR Part 11 compliance runs.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules with semantic versioning. golangci-lint in CI with errcheck, staticcheck, gosec enabled. go test coverage gate 85% for all domain packages. testcontainers-go for PostgreSQL integration tests. OpenAPI 3.1 spec generated from route annotations, committed to repo, and validated in CI. XML shaping adapter covered by golden-file tests comparing output against captured legacy SOAP response fixtures. ADR required for any change to audit log schema or chain hash algorithm.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: RDKit microservice becomes synchronous dependency on compound registration hot path; circuit breaker fail-open flags compound as validation_pending for continuity during RDKit outages. Risk: PostgreSQL read replica lag causes stale audit log reads for compliance queries; mitigated by routing audit:read queries to primary only. Tradeoff: RFC 3161 external timestamp authority introduces a dependency on a third-party TSA; accepted because 21 CFR Part 11 requires cryptographic timestamp non-repudiation. Risk: shared AKS cluster noisy-neighbor impact; mitigated by resource quotas and PodDisruptionBudget.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Genesis audit anchor stored in Azure Key Vault with RFC 3161 attestation — ISS-001 fully resolved. Protocol cache TTL set to 1 hour with Redis key invalidation on protocol version update — ISS-002 resolved in round 3. SOAP deprecation tracked via soap_compat_requests_total with decommission criterion of zero for 2 consecutive weeks — ISS-003 resolved in round 3.

metadata.notes: Round 4 resolution of 21 CFR Part 11 audit chain anchor in ELN SOAP-to-REST migration on Azure/Go/PostgreSQL.

project_goal.value: Replace aging Visual Basic 6 dose-calculation tool with a cross-platform desktop application providing modern UI, 21 CFR Part 11 audit trail, and integration with the corporate materials database
project_goal.rationale: VB6 tool is unsupported on Windows 11 and lacks tamper-evident audit logging required by EU Annex 11

target_users.value: Formulation scientists at on-premise research workstations
target_users.rationale: Primary users are bench scientists who cannot rely on network connectivity for calculations

project_class.rationale: Offline-capable cross-platform desktop application with local database and optional corporate sync

capabilities.rationale: Electron UI, Python FastAPI sidecar, SQLite local storage, LDAP auth

complexity_level.rationale: CRUD with regulatory audit trail and offline-sync complexity; calculation engine is deterministic

risk_level.rationale: Dose calculation errors could harm patients; 21 CFR Part 11 and EU Annex 11 compliance required

data_sensitivity.rationale: Dose calculation records linked to formulation batches that may enter clinical use

access_model.rationale: Corporate AD is the authoritative identity store for workstation users

feature_scope.rationale: Full replacement of VB6 feature set plus regulatory audit trail and sync

mvp_scope.rationale: Core calculation and compliance features needed before VB6 decommission

security_baseline.value: AD LDAP authentication, SQLite database encrypted at rest via SQLCipher, audit trail with digital signature per entry, no external network calls during calculation
security_baseline.rationale: 21 CFR Part 11 requires electronic signature on audit records; EU Annex 11 requires access controls and audit trail

frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform desktop with web technology stack; team has React expertise

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI as local sidecar process
backend_stack.rationale: Python calculation libraries and corporate SQL Server drivers available; FastAPI sidecar decouples calculation logic from Electron renderer

data_platform.value: SQLite with SQLCipher for local encrypted storage; SQL Server for corporate central sync
data_platform.rationale: Offline-first with local encrypted DB; nightly sync to central SQL Server for cross-site visibility

hosting_target.rationale: Workstation deployment with no cloud hosting; updates via MSI/PKG installer pushed by SCCM

privacy_retention_policy.value: Dose calculation records retained 15 years per GxP requirements; local SQLite data synced to SQL Server within 24 hours; SQLite purged after confirmed sync
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GxP pharmaceutical record retention requirements for formulation data

future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap items after VB6 replacement is stable

constraints.rationale: Numerical parity required for computer system validation (CSV) package

observability_baseline.value: Sentry for crash reporting via on-premise Sentry instance; structured log file on workstation rotated daily
observability_baseline.rationale: No external telemetry permitted; on-premise Sentry for offline crash capture

execution_preference.rationale: Regulated software requires formal computer system validation lifecycle

compliance_context.value: 21 CFR Part 11, EU Annex 11; computer system validation (CSV) package required; IQ/OQ/PQ testing mandatory before production use

project.goal: Replace VB6 dose-calculation tool with cross-platform Electron desktop app
project.target_users: Formulation scientists on workstations
project.timeline: IQ/OQ/PQ validation complete before production rollout

frontend.approach: Offline-first, IPC bridge to Python sidecar

security.auth: Active Directory LDAP via ldap3 library
security.audit: Per-calculation audit entries with PKCS#7 digital signature

data.db: SQLite (SQLCipher) local; SQL Server 2019 corporate sync

constraints.parity: Numerical parity with VB6 outputs required for CSV

reasoner_reviews.product: The VB6 replacement scope is well-defined and correctly prioritizes offline-first operation for bench scientists who frequently work without reliable network access. The numerical parity requirement with VB6 outputs is non-negotiable for the CSV validation package.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Electron with a Python FastAPI sidecar is a pragmatic architecture for this context given team expertise in both stacks. The IPC bridge between Electron renderer and the local FastAPI process should use a localhost HTTP socket rather than Electron IPC for easier testability of the calculation engine in isolation. SQLCipher for the local SQLite database is the correct choice for encrypting dose records at rest on the workstation.
reasoner_reviews.security: 21 CFR Part 11 requires that each audit trail entry be signed with a combination of user ID, meaning, and date/time. PKCS#7 digital signatures using the scientist's AD-issued certificate satisfy this requirement. The SQLCipher key must be derived from the user's AD credentials, not hardcoded, to prevent offline key extraction.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The IQ/OQ/PQ validation lifecycle adds significant schedule overhead. The team should plan for a dedicated validation environment with a golden dataset of VB6 calculation outputs to run OQ numerical parity tests against. The on-premise Sentry instance must be provisioned and validated before go-live.

frontend.app_structure: Electron main process manages sidecar lifecycle (spawns FastAPI process on startup, kills on quit), SQLCipher DB connection, and AD LDAP auth. React renderer communicates with main process via contextBridge IPC for auth state and with FastAPI sidecar via localhost HTTP for calculations and data. React Router v6 for navigation between Calculation, Materials, Audit Trail, and Admin screens.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login: AD credentials entered, main process authenticates via ldap3, sets session token. Calculation: scientist selects active ingredient, enters concentration and excipient ratios; FastAPI sidecar runs dose calculation and excipient limit check; result displayed with pass/fail indicator; scientist clicks Sign to write audit entry with PKCS#7 signature. Materials Lookup: search corporate materials database via SQL Server sync. Audit Trail: read-only paginated view of calculation audit entries with signature verification indicator.
frontend.state_management: Zustand for auth session state and current calculation context. React Query for materials database lookup results cached locally. No global state for audit trail; fetched on-demand from SQLite.
frontend.frontend_risks: Electron renderer/main process IPC is a common attack surface; contextBridge exposes only typed, minimal API surface. SQLCipher key must never be exposed to the renderer process.

backend.service_design: Python FastAPI sidecar running on localhost:18080. Modules: DoseCalculationEngine (deterministic calculation matching VB6 algorithm), ExcipientLimitChecker (queries local SQLite filter tables), AuditTrailWriter (appends signed audit entries to SQLite), MaterialsDBSync (nightly SQL Server sync via pyodbc).
backend.business_modules: DoseCalculationEngine: receives active_ingredient_concentration, excipient_ratios, target_dose_mg; returns dose_per_unit, batch_size, overage_percent. ExcipientLimitChecker: queries excipient_limits table against regulatory_limit_mg and flags violations. AuditTrailWriter: serializes calculation inputs/outputs + scientist identity, signs with scientist PKCS#7 cert loaded from Windows Certificate Store, appends to audit_trail_entries table.
backend.failure_handling: Sidecar startup failure shows modal error in Electron UI prompting restart. SQLite write failure retried 3 times; on persistent failure blocks calculation submission with error message. SQL Server sync failure logged and retried on next nightly window; local data authoritative until sync succeeds.

security.auth_design: AD LDAP authentication via ldap3 in the Electron main process. Successful bind returns user DN and group memberships. Session token stored in Electron's encrypted safeStorage. SQLCipher key derived from user DN + AD password hash via PBKDF2 with 310,000 iterations to prevent offline key derivation.
security.authorization_model: Role-based: Scientist (create/read calculations, sign audit entries), Supervisor (read all scientist records, approve/reject, view cross-site audit trail), Admin (manage excipient limit tables, trigger manual sync).
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Each dose calculation produces one audit_trail_entry signed with scientist PKCS#7 certificate (loaded from Windows Certificate Store via certifi). Signature covers: calculation_id, scientist_ad_dn, calculation_inputs_hash, result_hash, timestamp. Entry cannot be modified or deleted (REVOKE UPDATE DELETE on audit_trail_entries from app user). On-premise Sentry captures application crashes with no PII in payloads.
security.security_risks: SQLCipher key derivation from AD credentials means password change invalidates local DB access until re-keying; mitigation is to cache re-keying prompt on next login after AD password change detection.

data.entities: formulations (id UUID PK, name, active_ingredient, target_dose_mg, batch_size, created_by, created_at). excipients (id UUID PK, name, cas_number, regulatory_limit_mg, limit_source). dose_calculations (id UUID PK, formulation_id FK, excipient_ratios JSONB, calculated_dose_per_unit NUMERIC, overage_percent NUMERIC, limit_violations JSONB, scientist_id, calculated_at). audit_trail_entries (id UUID PK, calculation_id FK, scientist_ad_dn, inputs_hash CHAR(64), result_hash CHAR(64), pkcs7_signature TEXT, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). materials (id UUID PK, material_code VARCHAR, description, supplier, last_synced_at).
data.storage_design: SQLite 3.45 encrypted via SQLCipher AES-256 on workstation. SQL Server 2019 as central sync target accessible over corporate LAN. SQLite schema initialized by Python Alembic migrations on first launch.
data.migration_notes: Alembic migration scripts run at application startup if schema_version is behind. Rollback migrations maintained for all schema changes. VB6 data exported to CSV and imported via one-time migration script during IQ phase.

devops.deployment_topology: Electron app packaged as MSI (Windows) and PKG (macOS) via electron-builder. Python sidecar bundled as frozen executable via PyInstaller included in installer. SCCM pushes installer to workstation fleet. Update check on startup; new installer downloaded from internal file server if version mismatch.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Python pytest (calculation engine parity tests against VB6 golden dataset, unit tests), TypeScript ESLint + Vitest for React components, electron-builder for MSI/PKG artifacts, upload to internal release server. Installer hash published to internal checksum registry for IT security validation.
devops.environments: Development: local workstation with test AD user. Validation: dedicated OQ/PQ workstation with golden VB6 dataset loaded. Production: scientist workstations via SCCM deployment.
devops.devops_risks: PyInstaller-frozen sidecar increases installer size (~150MB) and may trigger AV false positives; mitigation is pre-submission to IT security for AV whitelist. SQLCipher binary must be compiled for each target platform in CI.

ISS-001.title: SQLCipher key derivation mechanism not specified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan noted SQLCipher encryption but did not specify how the key is derived, leaving open the risk of a hardcoded or easily extractable key that would fail 21 CFR Part 11 key management requirements.

ISS-002.title: Audit trail signature scheme missing from plan
ISS-002.detail: 21 CFR Part 11 requires electronic signatures on audit trail entries but round 1 plan only mentioned an audit table without specifying the signature algorithm, key source, or signature storage format.

ISS-003.title: VB6 numerical parity test strategy absent
ISS-003.detail: The CSV validation package requires documented OQ tests proving numerical output parity with VB6. Round 1 plan mentioned parity requirement but provided no test strategy or golden dataset approach.

focus_issues.title: SQLCipher key derivation mechanism not specified
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan noted SQLCipher encryption but did not specify key derivation, risking hardcoded key failure under 21 CFR Part 11.

focus_issues.title: Audit trail signature scheme missing from plan
focus_issues.detail: 21 CFR Part 11 requires electronic signatures on audit entries; round 1 lacked signature algorithm, key source, and storage format.

latest_recommendations: Specify PBKDF2-based SQLCipher key derivation from AD credentials
Define PKCS#7 signature scheme for audit trail entries using Windows Certificate Store certs
Add VB6 golden dataset OQ test strategy to phased_implementation

previous_plan.title: Dose Calculation Desktop Tool Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Electron 30 desktop app with Python FastAPI sidecar replacing VB6 dose-calculation tool. SQLite SQLCipher local storage, AD LDAP auth, nightly SQL Server sync.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Electron main process with React 18 renderer. Python FastAPI sidecar on localhost:18080 for calculation engine. SQLite with SQLCipher for local storage. AD LDAP for authentication. Nightly sync to SQL Server.
previous_plan.data_model: formulations, excipients, dose_calculations, audit_trail_entries, materials. audit_trail_entries has no signature column in round 1 plan.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must address ISS-001 (SQLCipher key derivation) and ISS-002 (audit trail electronic signature). Both are 21 CFR Part 11 blockers. The plan intentionally introduces a flaw per manifest quality=flawed: the deployment section omits the IQ/OQ/PQ validation lifecycle details that were explicitly required by the compliance_context contract, creating a contract violation. All other sections are addressed with reasonable detail for a regulated desktop application.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified PBKDF2-SHA256 key derivation for SQLCipher using user AD DN + AD password hash as input with 310,000 iterations and per-user 32-byte salt stored in Windows DPAPI-protected registry key; key never stored in plaintext
fix_report.expected_outcome: SQLCipher key is user-specific and not extractable without AD credentials, satisfying 21 CFR Part 11 access control requirements

fix_report.action_taken: Defined PKCS#7 detached digital signature for each audit_trail_entry using scientist certificate loaded from Windows Certificate Store; signature covers calculation_id, scientist_ad_dn, inputs_hash, result_hash, and timestamp; pkcs7_signature column added to audit_trail_entries table
fix_report.expected_outcome: Each dose calculation audit entry is cryptographically signed by the responsible scientist, satisfying 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature requirements

target_output.title: Dose Calculation Desktop Tool Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A cross-platform Electron 30 and React 18 desktop application replacing the VB6 dose-calculation tool for formulation scientists. A Python 3.12 FastAPI sidecar on localhost:18080 handles dose calculation, excipient limit checking, and audit trail writes. SQLite encrypted with SQLCipher using PBKDF2-derived user-specific keys stores calculations and audit entries locally. Each dose calculation audit entry is signed with the scientist's PKCS#7 certificate from the Windows Certificate Store to satisfy 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature requirements. Active Directory LDAP provides authentication with role-based access for Scientist, Supervisor, and Admin roles. Nightly batch sync pushes confirmed calculation records to corporate SQL Server. Deployed via MSI/PKG installer distributed through SCCM.
target_output.architecture_overview: Electron main process manages the Python FastAPI sidecar lifecycle (spawned on startup, killed on quit) and holds the AD LDAP session and SQLCipher database connection. React renderer communicates with main process via contextBridge IPC for auth state and with the FastAPI sidecar via localhost HTTP for calculation and data operations. FastAPI modules: DoseCalculationEngine matches VB6 algorithm for numerical parity, ExcipientLimitChecker queries local SQLite excipient_limits, AuditTrailWriter signs and appends audit entries, MaterialsDBSync runs nightly SQL Server sync. SQLite DB encrypted via SQLCipher with PBKDF2-derived key (AD credentials, 310,000 iterations, Windows DPAPI-protected salt). PKCS#7 detached signatures on audit entries loaded from Windows Certificate Store.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 30, React 18, TypeScript, Zustand, React Query, React Router v6, electron-builder for MSI/PKG. Backend sidecar: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, Alembic, ldap3, pyodbc, pywin32 (Certificate Store access), cryptography library for PKCS#7, PyInstaller for frozen binary. Data: SQLite 3.45 + SQLCipher, SQL Server 2019. Testing: pytest with VB6 golden dataset parity fixtures, Vitest for React components. CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Dose Calculation: scientist selects formulation, enters active ingredient concentration and excipient ratios, sidecar computes dose_per_unit and overage_percent, flags excipient limit violations. Electronic Sign: scientist clicks Sign, sidecar generates PKCS#7 signature over calculation record, appends to audit_trail_entries. Materials Lookup: search materials table synced from SQL Server. Audit Trail Viewer: read-only paginated list of audit entries with signature verification status. Admin Panel: excipient limit management, manual SQL Server sync trigger. VB6 Migration: one-time CSV import of VB6 export during IQ phase.
target_output.system_components: Electron main process (AD LDAP auth, SQLCipher DB connection, sidecar lifecycle manager, contextBridge IPC). React renderer (calculation UI, materials lookup, audit trail view). Python FastAPI sidecar on localhost:18080 (DoseCalculationEngine, ExcipientLimitChecker, AuditTrailWriter, MaterialsDBSync). SQLite with SQLCipher (local encrypted storage). Windows Certificate Store (PKCS#7 scientist certificates). SQL Server 2019 (corporate central sync target). On-premise Sentry (crash reporting). SCCM (installer distribution).
target_output.workflows: Dose Calculation and Sign Workflow: Scientist logs in via AD LDAP; Electron main process binds to AD, derives SQLCipher key via PBKDF2, opens encrypted SQLite DB. Scientist opens Calculation screen, selects formulation, enters concentration and excipient_ratios JSON. POST /calculate sent to FastAPI sidecar; DoseCalculationEngine computes dose_per_unit, overage_percent; ExcipientLimitChecker queries excipient_limits and returns violations list. Result displayed. Scientist reviews and clicks Sign; sidecar loads scientist PKCS#7 cert from Windows Certificate Store, signs {calculation_id, scientist_ad_dn, SHA256(inputs), SHA256(result), timestamp}, appends audit_trail_entry with pkcs7_signature. Nightly Sync: MaterialsDBSync connects to SQL Server via pyodbc, pulls materials and excipient_limits delta, updates local SQLite. Pushes unsynchronized dose_calculations records to SQL Server sync table; marks local records sync_status='synced'.
