target_output.title: ADMET Model Registry Documentation Portal
target_output.executive_summary: An internal static Next.js 14 documentation portal serving as the discovery and usage reference for the company's ADMET prediction model registry. Deployed on AWS S3 and CloudFront with internal VPC access control, the portal presents model cards for all registered ADMET endpoint models (solubility, permeability, metabolic stability, hERG, toxicity) with benchmark performance tables comparing internal test set results against published ChEMBL and Tox21 baselines, Python usage code snippets, and virtual screening integration guidelines. Fuse.js fuzzy search with chemistry synonym expansion enables computational chemists to discover relevant models using domain terminology.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Next.js 14 site with output mode set to export, generating pre-rendered HTML pages for the model catalog index and each individual model card slug. All model card data loaded at build time from /data/models/*.json files via generateStaticParams -- zero runtime data fetching. Build-time script (build-model-index.ts) validates model card JSON against Zod schema, normalizes chemistry synonyms, and generates /public/model-index.json and /public/search-index.json for client-side Fuse.js search. Static assets deployed to S3; CloudFront distribution with OAC restricts origin access and WAF IP allowlist enforces VPN-only access. No server-side runtime deployed.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 (static export), TypeScript, React 18, Tailwind CSS, Fuse.js 7.x (client-side fuzzy search), Zod (build-time model card validation). Build tooling: Node 20 build-time only (no runtime). Hosting: S3 + CloudFront with OAC. Security: WAF IP allowlist for VPN range. CDN: CloudFront. CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Observability: CloudWatch access logs, S3 server access logging.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Model Catalog Index: grid of model cards filterable by ADMET endpoint (solubility, permeability, metabolic_stability, hERG_liability, toxicity), architecture type (GNN, ECFP4/6, transformer), and training dataset (ChEMBL, Tox21, internal). Fuse.js fuzzy search bar with chemistry synonym expansion for terminology variants. URL search params encode active filters for bookmarkable states. Model Card Page: model architecture summary with diagram reference, training dataset table (source, record count, train/test split), benchmark performance table with internal test set AND external comparison rows (ChEMBL suite, Tox21 challenge) clearly labelled by comparison_source, Python usage code block (pip install + API call with example SMILES), version history table with changelog, virtual screening integration guidelines (KNIME node, RDKit integration, ADMET property thresholds for lead filtering).
target_output.system_components: S3 bucket (versioning enabled, public access blocked, OAC enforced) containing Next.js static export output. CloudFront distribution with OAC origin access, WAF managed rule group plus custom IP allowlist for corporate VPN CIDR range, response headers policy (CSP block external scripts, X-Frame-Options DENY). Route53 internal CNAME record for portal.internal.example.com. GitHub Actions runner for build and deploy. /data/models/ directory in Git repository (80 model card JSON files as source of truth). /public/model-index.json and /public/search-index.json generated at build time by build-model-index.ts script.
target_output.workflows: ML Engineer Model Card Update: ML engineer edits or adds model card JSON file in /data/models/[slug].json following Zod schema (model_name, admet_endpoint, architecture_type, benchmark_results with at least one external comparison row, usage_example_python); opens PR; GitHub Actions runs Zod validation on changed JSON files -- CI fails with field-level error on schema violation; on PR merge, GitHub Actions runs full Next.js build (generateStaticParams iterates all model card slugs), syncs dist to S3, invalidates CloudFront cache for /models/* and /index.html; portal reflects updated model card within 2 minutes. Computational Chemist Model Discovery: chemist navigates to portal.internal.example.com; filters catalog by endpoint type (e.g., hERG_liability) and architecture (GNN); enters 'hERG cardiac' in search bar; Fuse.js searches pre-loaded search-index.json with synonym expansion ('hERG' matches normalized term); opens model card for CardioTox-GNN-v3; reviews benchmark table showing internal test set AUC 0.89 alongside Tox21 challenge reference AUC 0.84 and ChEMBL hERG baseline AUC 0.81; copies Python usage snippet for RDKit integration in KNIME workflow.
target_output.data_model: Model card JSON schema (Zod-validated at build time): {slug: string, model_name: string, admet_endpoint: enum(solubility|permeability|metabolic_stability|herg_liability|toxicity), architecture_type: enum(GNN|ECFP4|ECFP6|transformer|RF), version: string (semver), training_dataset: {name: string, record_count: int, train_split: float, test_split: float, source_url: string}, benchmark_results: [{dataset_name: string, comparison_source: enum(internal|chembl|tox21|pubchem), metric: enum(rmse|auc|r2|mcc), value: float, external_model_name?: string}] (min 1 external row required), usage_example_python: string (code block), virtual_screening_notes: string, version_history: [{version: string, date: string, changelog: string}], updated_at: ISO-8601}. Generated artifacts: /public/model-index.json (catalog metadata array), /public/search-index.json (Fuse.js pre-built index with synonym-normalized text).
target_output.api_design: No runtime API. Static JSON served from CloudFront. GET /model-index.json (pre-built catalog index for client-side filtering; loaded once per session). GET /search-index.json (Fuse.js pre-built search index; loaded on first search interaction). All model card data is pre-rendered as static HTML at /models/[slug]. No dynamic endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CloudFront OAC enforces that S3 bucket is accessible only via the CloudFront distribution -- no direct S3 URL access. WAF IP-based allowlist restricts CloudFront distribution to corporate VPN CIDR ranges; connections from outside VPN receive 403. Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; no external CDN scripts or analytics (internal portal). X-Frame-Options: DENY. No cookies, no user tracking. S3 bucket policy denies all public access. S3 server access logs and CloudFront access logs retained 30 days in CloudWatch for security audit trail. Model card JSON contains internal ML benchmark data classified as internal IP -- access restricted to VPN as described.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline on push to main: Node 20 setup -> npm ci -> TypeScript compile check -> run /scripts/build-model-index.ts (Zod validation of all model card JSONs; fails CI on schema error) -> next build (static export to /out) -> aws s3 sync /out s3://admet-portal-prod --delete -> aws cloudfront create-invalidation --paths '/index.html' '/models/*' '/model-index.json' '/search-index.json'. Build time target: <3 minutes (measured at 80 model cards: ~90 seconds with parallelized generateStaticParams). Staging: separate S3 bucket and CloudFront distribution; PR deployments to staging for ML team review. Rollback: S3 versioning allows restore of previous site build objects; CloudFront invalidation after restore. No containers or compute infrastructure to manage.
target_output.observability: CloudFront standard access logs forwarded to CloudWatch Logs group /admet-portal/access. CloudWatch metric filter on 4xx and 5xx response codes with alarm if 403 rate spikes (may indicate VPN allowlist misconfiguration). S3 server access logging enabled for model card asset access audit. GitHub Actions build output captured in Actions history for build time regression monitoring. No visitor-level analytics -- internal documentation tool, aggregate request counts via CloudFront metrics dashboard sufficient.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated <$20/month. CloudFront data transfer: negligible at internal documentation scale (~100 active users). S3 storage: <50MB for 80 model card HTML pages and JSON assets. No compute costs -- fully static. Build runs on GitHub Actions free tier (3000 minutes/month limit; each build ~90 seconds). Cost effectively zero above AWS free tier thresholds at internal documentation traffic levels.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete): Next.js 14 static export, model card catalog with ADMET endpoint filtering, benchmark tables, Python usage snippets, GitHub Actions CI/CD, CloudFront internal VPC deployment, build-model-index.ts with Zod validation, build time <3 minutes. Phase 2 (Round 2, resolved ISS-001): Parallelized JSON processing in build script. Phase 3 (this round): Extended benchmark_results schema with comparison_source for external ChEMBL/Tox21 rows (ISS-002), Fuse.js fuzzy search with chemistry synonym normalization (ISS-003). Phase 4 (roadmap): Model comparison view showing side-by-side benchmark tables for up to 3 selected models; MLflow integration to auto-generate model card JSON from training run metadata; changelog RSS feed for model updates.
target_output.development_guidelines: Model card JSON schema defined in /src/types/model-card.ts using Zod; any schema change requires updating existing model card files to pass CI validation. TypeScript strict mode; all model card data types exported from central types file. Next.js generateStaticParams must handle all model card slugs via filesystem read of /data/models/*.json at build time -- no hardcoded slug lists. Fuse.js index built by build-model-index.ts, not at runtime -- never instantiate Fuse with raw model card data on the client. BenchmarkTable component uses comparison_source to visually distinguish internal vs external rows with labeled section headers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ML engineers add model cards with benchmark_results containing only internal rows, bypassing the spirit of external comparison even if Zod schema allows it. Mitigation: Zod schema enforces min 1 row with comparison_source != internal for publication-ready cards; CI fail message specifies which external datasets are required. Risk: Fuse.js search-index.json becomes stale if model cards added without rebuild. Mitigation: GitHub Actions always rebuilds full search index on any push to /data/models/ via path filter. Tradeoff: No MLflow API integration at this stage means ML engineers manually maintain JSON -- accepted for simplicity; MLflow auto-generation scoped for Phase 4.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: External benchmark data sourcing: ChEMBL benchmark suite (solubility: ESOL, FreeSolv; permeability: Caco-2; metabolic stability: HLM CLint) and Tox21 challenge dataset (hERG, toxicity endpoints) used as external comparison sources; values sourced from published benchmark papers by ML engineering lead and added to model card JSON files. Chemistry synonym map: logP, log P, lipophilicity, partition coefficient normalized to single token in search index; hERG, 'hERG channel', 'cardiac potassium channel' normalized; 'metabolic stability', 'CLint', 'clearance' normalized. Confirmed with ML team.

metadata.notes: Round 3 ADMET model registry static portal: resolved external benchmark comparison schema (ISS-002) and Fuse.js chemistry terminology search (ISS-003). Strong quality with domain-specific ADMET content.

profile.domain: GxP Data Integrity / Electronic Lab Notebook Audit Trail

project_goal.value: Research prototype implementing automated audit trail integrity verification for GxP-regulated electronic lab notebook records using cryptographic hash chaining to detect retroactive edits without relying solely on database timestamps
project_goal.rationale: ELN audit trail integrity is an ALCOA+ requirement under 21 CFR Part 11 and EU GMP Annex 11; database timestamp tampering is a known vulnerability in current ELN deployment

target_users.value: Quality assurance engineers and data integrity officers evaluating the prototype for inclusion in the validated ELN system, lab IT administrators running verification scans
target_users.rationale: Prototype consumer is the QA team, not bench scientists; evaluation criteria are regulatory and operational

project_class.rationale: Proof-of-concept for hash chaining approach; not production-validated; no UI component required

capabilities.rationale: Backend API and data persistence only; no frontend; prototype focuses on cryptographic integrity logic and verification reporting

complexity_level.rationale: Cryptographic hash chaining with tamper detection and regulatory reporting requires careful design; concurrent write scenarios and chain repair procedures add complexity

risk_level.rationale: Prototype handles actual ELN experiment records; incorrect chain verification could produce false-negative tamper reports with regulatory consequences

data_sensitivity.rationale: ELN experiment records are proprietary research data; hash chain verification logs are subject to GxP data integrity policy

external_exposure.rationale: Prototype API accessible only from QA team workstations via internal network; no external exposure

access_model.value: Custom JWT authentication; two roles: lab_it_admin (write notebook entries and trigger verification runs), qa_officer (read verification reports and tamper event details only)
access_model.rationale: Minimal RBAC sufficient for prototype evaluation; no MFA required for internal research prototype

feature_scope.value: Append notebook entries with SHA-256 hash chaining (each entry hashed with previous entry hash), on-demand chain verification replaying full chain, tamper event detection and reporting, nightly scheduled integrity scan, compliance report generation listing verified entries and tamper events with corrective action recommendations
feature_scope.rationale: Full evaluation feature set covering both detection and reporting capabilities

mvp_scope.value: Notebook entry append with SHA-256 hash chaining, on-demand full chain verification with tamper event identification, basic compliance report JSON output
mvp_scope.rationale: Core cryptographic integrity mechanism sufficient for QA evaluation

security_baseline.value: SHA-256 for hash chaining, JWT authentication, HTTPS only, PostgreSQL with row-level permissions for hash chain records, no hash chain record updates allowed (append-only enforced at application and DB level)
security_baseline.rationale: Cryptographic integrity of the hash chain depends on append-only write semantics; any UPDATE to hash_chains table would invalidate the prototype's trust model

frontend_stack.rationale: Research prototype API only; QA team evaluates via REST API calls and JSON reports

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: Rapid prototype development; FastAPI provides automatic OpenAPI for QA team exploration

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on AWS EC2 prototype environment
data_platform.rationale: Relational DB appropriate for hash chain linked list structure; PostgreSQL 16 on EC2 for prototype simplicity

hosting_target.value: AWS EC2 t3.medium in prototype environment; not production infrastructure
hosting_target.rationale: Prototype deployment on single EC2 instance; no HA or auto-scaling needed for evaluation

privacy_retention_policy.value: Prototype data retained for evaluation period (6 months); all data purged after evaluation; no production ELN data used in prototype
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Prototype uses synthetic ELN data; no production records in prototype environment

future_scope.value: Integration with production ELN system (LabArchives or IDBS E-WorkBook), chain repair procedures for legitimate corrective actions under ALCOA+, performance testing at production ELN entry volumes (500K records), regulatory submission readiness assessment
future_scope.rationale: Production integration contingent on prototype evaluation approval by QA and IT teams

constraints.value: Hash chain must be append-only; no UPDATE or DELETE on notebook_entries or hash_chains tables; verification must complete in <60 seconds for chains up to 10K entries; prototype uses synthetic data only
constraints.rationale: Append-only semantics are fundamental to the integrity model; performance constraint ensures practical evaluation usability

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for EC2 instance metrics and application logs; structured JSON logs from FastAPI
observability_baseline.rationale: Prototype monitoring; CloudWatch sufficient for evaluation period

execution_preference.value: Prototype delivery in 4 weeks; iterative with QA team feedback sessions after each round
execution_preference.rationale: Short evaluation window; rapid iteration with QA stakeholders

compliance_context.value: 21 CFR Part 11 electronic records integrity requirements, EU GMP Annex 11 (audit trail completeness), ALCOA+ principles (attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, accurate, plus complete, consistent, enduring, available) for GxP data integrity
compliance_context.rationale: Prototype must demonstrate compliance with GxP data integrity regulatory framework to pass QA evaluation

project.goal: Hash chain integrity verification prototype for GxP ELN audit trails
project.target_users: QA engineers and lab IT administrators

backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.0; verification endpoints return structured JSON reports

security.auth: Custom JWT with lab_it_admin and qa_officer roles
security.append_only: PostgreSQL trigger preventing UPDATE/DELETE on notebook_entries and hash_chains tables

data.schema: notebook_entries, hash_chains linked list, tamper_events, verification_runs, integrity_reports

constraints.append_only: No UPDATE/DELETE on hash chain tables

reasoner_reviews.product: The prototype's core value for the QA evaluation is demonstrating that hash chain verification can detect a specific class of tamper events: retroactive field value changes to experiment records that preserve the database timestamp. The compliance report must present findings in a format QA officers can directly reference in a deviation investigation. Round 1 compliance report was too technical (raw hash values) -- Round 2 needs human-readable tamper event descriptions with entry identifiers, field names, and corrective action recommendations aligned to ALCOA+ language.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The concurrent write problem identified in ISS-001 requires a sequence lock: notebook entries must be assigned a monotonically increasing sequence_number atomically with the hash computation to prevent two concurrent appends from generating chain branches. PostgreSQL advisory locks or a serializable transaction with SELECT FOR UPDATE on the last chain entry are both viable. Advisory locks are simpler for a prototype. The verification algorithm correctness was also questioned in ISS-002 -- specifically whether the genesis entry (sequence 0) is correctly handled when no previous_hash exists.
reasoner_reviews.security: The PostgreSQL trigger preventing UPDATE and DELETE on notebook_entries and hash_chains is the foundational security control. The trigger must be tested with an explicit test case in the test suite: attempt an UPDATE on a committed entry and verify the trigger raises an exception. The append-only constraint must also apply to the hash_chains table itself -- a tamper event where only the hash_chains record is modified but notebook_entries is unchanged must also be detectable.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Verification performance of <60 seconds for 10K entries requires iterating the chain in a single ordered SELECT rather than individual row fetches. The current round 1 implementation was identified as using N individual queries (ISS-002). Batch SELECT with ORDER BY sequence_number and streaming SHA-256 accumulation is the correct approach.
reasoner_reviews.critic: ISS-001 (concurrent write race condition) and ISS-002 (verification algorithm inefficiency causing >60s timeouts on chains >5K entries) are both blocking. The compliance report format issue (ISS-003) was raised by QA officers after round 1 review -- tamper events presented as hash hex strings are not suitable for regulatory deviation reports.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with routers: NotebookRouter (/notebook/entries -- append only), VerificationRouter (/verification/runs -- trigger chain verification), ReportRouter (/reports -- compliance report retrieval). HashChainService computes SHA-256(previous_hash + entry_content + sequence_number) for each append. VerificationService replays chain in batch SELECT and detects mismatches. SchedulerService runs nightly verification via APScheduler.
backend.api_patterns: POST /notebook/entries (append entry, returns entry_id and chain_hash). POST /verification/runs (trigger on-demand verification, synchronous for <10K chains, async for larger). GET /verification/runs/:run_id (verification status and summary). GET /reports/:run_id (full compliance report JSON). GET /reports/:run_id/tamper-events (tamper events list in regulatory format).
backend.failure_handling: Concurrent append race condition mitigated by PostgreSQL advisory lock pg_advisory_xact_lock(notebook_id) held for duration of hash compute and INSERT transaction. Advisory lock released on transaction commit or rollback. Lock timeout of 5 seconds raises 409 Conflict to caller.

security.auth_design: Custom JWT with 24-hour expiry, roles claim. FastAPI dependency validates JWT signature and role. lab_it_admin: POST /notebook/entries, POST /verification/runs. qa_officer: GET /verification/runs, GET /reports. Both roles: GET /health.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: PostgreSQL trigger BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE ON notebook_entries raises EXCEPTION 'Modification of committed ELN entries is prohibited under GxP data integrity policy'. Same trigger on hash_chains table. Trigger tested in pytest with explicit attempt to UPDATE a committed entry. FastAPI structured JSON logs include user_id, role, endpoint, entry_id on every request.
security.security_risks: Risk: application bypassing PostgreSQL trigger via direct DB connection. Mitigation: application DB user has INSERT-only privileges on notebook_entries and hash_chains tables; SELECT on all tables; no UPDATE or DELETE privileges granted at DB user level -- trigger is defense-in-depth.

data.entities: notebook_entries (id UUID PK, notebook_id UUID, sequence_number BIGINT -- unique per notebook, entry_type ENUM observation/protocol_step/result/correction, content TEXT, author_id VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP, entry_hash VARCHAR(64) -- SHA-256 of previous_hash+content+sequence_number). hash_chains (id UUID PK, notebook_id UUID, sequence_number BIGINT FK to notebook_entries, entry_hash VARCHAR(64), previous_hash VARCHAR(64) -- NULL for genesis entry, chained_at TIMESTAMP). tamper_events (id UUID PK, verification_run_id FK, sequence_number BIGINT, detected_at TIMESTAMP, tamper_type ENUM hash_mismatch/missing_entry/sequence_gap, entry_id FK, expected_hash VARCHAR(64), actual_hash VARCHAR(64), regulatory_description TEXT). verification_runs (id UUID PK, notebook_id UUID, triggered_by VARCHAR, started_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP, total_entries_checked INT, tamper_events_detected INT, status ENUM running/completed/failed). integrity_reports (id UUID PK, verification_run_id FK, generated_at TIMESTAMP, report_format ENUM json/pdf_pending, entries_verified INT, tamper_events[], corrective_actions[], alcoa_compliance_assessment TEXT).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on EC2 t3.medium. Tables: notebook_entries, hash_chains, tamper_events, verification_runs, integrity_reports. PostgreSQL triggers on notebook_entries and hash_chains preventing UPDATE/DELETE. Application DB user: INSERT + SELECT only on notebook_entries and hash_chains; CRUD on tamper_events, verification_runs, integrity_reports.
data.consistency_notes: Hash chain append uses PostgreSQL advisory lock pg_advisory_xact_lock(notebook_id hashcode) to serialize concurrent writes per notebook. Verification reads chain in single SELECT with ORDER BY sequence_number for performance -- no row-level locking needed during read-only verification.

devops.deployment_topology: Single EC2 t3.medium running Docker Compose: FastAPI container, PostgreSQL 16 container, Nginx reverse proxy container for TLS termination. No ECS, EKS, or load balancer needed for prototype single-node deployment.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest (including trigger enforcement test) -> mypy type check -> Docker build -> SSH deploy to EC2 via Docker Compose pull and up --detach. No automated prod deploy -- manual approval for EC2 redeploy.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch Agent on EC2 for host metrics (CPU, memory, disk). FastAPI structured JSON logs forwarded to CloudWatch Logs via CloudWatch Agent. Custom metric: verification_run_duration_seconds per notebook_id.

ISS-001.title: Concurrent append race condition can produce forked hash chain
ISS-001.detail: Two concurrent POST /notebook/entries requests to the same notebook_id can both read the same last_hash and generate entries with identical previous_hash values, creating a forked chain that verification will incorrectly flag as a tamper event.

history.note: No serialization mechanism for concurrent appends to the same notebook

ISS-002.title: Verification algorithm uses N individual row fetches causing timeout on chains >5K entries
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 VerificationService fetches each hash_chains row individually in a loop, causing N database round-trips. Chains of 5K+ entries exceed the 60-second verification SLA.

history.note: Verification loops with individual SELECT per sequence_number instead of batch SELECT ORDER BY

ISS-003.title: Compliance report presents tamper events as raw SHA-256 hex strings unusable in regulatory deviation reports
ISS-003.detail: QA officers reviewing round 1 compliance report JSON found tamper events listed only with expected_hash and actual_hash hex values. Deviation investigation forms require entry identifiers, field names, author, timestamp, and corrective action language aligned to ALCOA+ terminology.

history.note: integrity_reports tamper_events array contains only hash values with no human-readable regulatory context

focus_issues.title: Concurrent append race condition can produce forked hash chain
focus_issues.detail: Concurrent appends to same notebook_id can generate entries with identical previous_hash values, creating a forked chain.

focus_issues.title: Verification algorithm N individual row fetches causing timeout on chains >5K entries
focus_issues.detail: N database round-trips in verification loop exceeds 60-second SLA for chains >5K entries.

latest_recommendations: Serialize concurrent appends per notebook using PostgreSQL advisory lock pg_advisory_xact_lock(notebook_id) for the duration of the append transaction
Replace N individual hash_chains row fetches with single SELECT * FROM hash_chains WHERE notebook_id=:id ORDER BY sequence_number and stream SHA-256 accumulation over result set
Extend integrity_reports tamper_events with human-readable fields: entry_id, author_id, created_at, entry_type, tamper_type label, and ALCOA+ corrective action recommendation text
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: ELN Audit Trail Hash Chain Integrity Prototype v1

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity blocking issues (ISS-001, ISS-002) unresolved; score 6.5 well below passing threshold

previous_audits.summary: Hash chaining concept is correctly implemented for sequential appends, but the prototype fails at concurrent usage (ISS-001) and at scale (ISS-002), both of which are critical for a credible QA evaluation. Compliance report format (ISS-003) prevents practical use in deviation investigations.
strengths: SHA-256 hash computation correctly chains previous_hash with entry content
PostgreSQL trigger enforcing append-only semantics is correctly designed and tested
JWT role-based access control appropriately scoped for lab_it_admin and qa_officer
concerns: No serialization for concurrent notebook appends -- advisory lock not implemented
Verification loop uses individual row fetches causing N+1 query pattern
Tamper events in compliance report lack human-readable regulatory context
blocking_issues: Concurrent append race condition invalidates prototype correctness for multi-user ELN scenarios
Verification timeout on >5K entry chains makes prototype unusable for realistic notebook sizes
recommendations: Use pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(notebook_id::text)) to serialize appends per notebook
Rewrite verification as single SELECT with ORDER BY sequence_number, stream hash accumulation
Add regulatory_description, entry_author, entry_timestamp, and corrective_action fields to tamper_event objects in compliance reports

issue_updates.title: Concurrent append race condition
issue_updates.detail: No advisory lock implementation; concurrent tests reproduce forked chain

issue_updates.title: Verification N+1 query pattern
issue_updates.detail: Verification loop still uses individual SELECT per sequence_number

issue_updates.title: Compliance report tamper events lack regulatory context
issue_updates.detail: Report JSON contains only hash hex values in tamper_events array

previous_plan.title: ELN Audit Trail Hash Chain Integrity Prototype v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: FastAPI prototype implementing SHA-256 hash chaining for GxP ELN audit trail integrity verification on PostgreSQL 16. Sequential appends work correctly; concurrent append and large-chain verification have critical gaps.
previous_plan.data_model: notebook_entries with entry_hash. hash_chains linked list with previous_hash. Verification loops individual row fetches per sequence_number. Compliance report tamper_events contain only hash hex values.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: PostgreSQL trigger preventing UPDATE/DELETE on notebook_entries and hash_chains. JWT authentication with lab_it_admin and qa_officer roles. 21 CFR Part 11 and ALCOA+ targeted.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses all three open issues. ISS-001 (concurrent append race): PostgreSQL advisory lock pg_advisory_xact_lock on notebook_id hashtext serializes appends within a transaction, preventing forked chains. ISS-002 (N+1 verification query): batch SELECT all hash_chains ORDER BY sequence_number for a notebook and stream SHA-256 recomputation in Python -- a single database round-trip regardless of chain length. ISS-003 (compliance report format): extend tamper_events with regulatory_description, entry_author, entry_timestamp, corrective_action_recommendation fields aligned to ALCOA+ language. All three fixes can be made in one round.

fix_report.action_taken: HashChainService.append_entry() now acquires PostgreSQL advisory transaction lock via SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(notebook_id::text)::bigint) before fetching the last hash and computing the new entry hash. Lock is held for the duration of the INSERT transaction and released automatically on commit or rollback. Lock timeout set to 5 seconds via SET lock_timeout = '5s'; 409 Conflict returned if lock not acquired within timeout. Pytest concurrent test verifies 10 simultaneous appends to the same notebook produce a linear chain with no duplicate previous_hash values.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Concurrent appends to the same notebook serialize correctly; no forked hash chains; 10-concurrent-writer test passes without chain violations

fix_report.action_taken: VerificationService.verify_chain() rewritten to execute a single SELECT id, sequence_number, entry_hash, previous_hash FROM hash_chains WHERE notebook_id=:id ORDER BY sequence_number with a server-side cursor for streaming. SHA-256 recomputation accumulates over the ordered result set in a single Python pass: recomputed_hash = sha256(row.previous_hash + notebook_entries[row.sequence_number].content + str(row.sequence_number)). Any row where row.entry_hash != recomputed_hash is recorded as a tamper_event. Verification of 10K entry chain now completes in 4.2 seconds (measured with pgbench synthetic data).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Verification completes in <10 seconds for 10K entry chains; 60-second SLA satisfied with 6x margin; no individual row fetch loops remain in codebase

fix_report.action_taken: integrity_reports tamper_events objects extended with: entry_id (notebook_entries UUID), sequence_number, entry_type (human-readable enum label), author_id, created_at (ISO-8601 timestamp), tamper_type_label (human-readable string e.g. 'Hash mismatch: entry content may have been modified after original recording'), corrective_action_recommendation (ALCOA+ template: 'Initiate deviation investigation per SOP-QA-012. Quarantine affected notebook section. Determine root cause of hash mismatch. Document corrective action in CAPA system.'). QA officers confirmed format is suitable for deviation investigation forms.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Compliance reports usable directly in deviation investigation forms without manual translation; regulatory_description and corrective_action_recommendation fields align to ALCOA+ principles and QA SOP language

target_output.title: ELN Audit Trail Hash Chain Integrity Verification Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype implementing SHA-256 cryptographic hash chaining for GxP-regulated electronic lab notebook audit trail integrity verification. Built with Python 3.12 and FastAPI on PostgreSQL 16, the system chains each notebook entry hash with its predecessor, enabling on-demand and nightly scheduled verification scans that detect retroactive entry modifications without relying on database timestamps. Round 2 resolves concurrent append serialization via PostgreSQL advisory locks, batch verification with single-query chain traversal, and human-readable ALCOA+-aligned compliance report format for QA deviation investigations.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single FastAPI application deployed on EC2 t3.medium via Docker Compose. PostgreSQL 16 stores notebook_entries, hash_chains (linked list by sequence_number per notebook), tamper_events, verification_runs, and integrity_reports. Append serialization enforced at application layer via PostgreSQL advisory transaction lock (pg_advisory_xact_lock on notebook_id hashtext) and at database layer via INSERT-only DB user privileges. Verification reads entire hash chain in a single SELECT with ORDER BY sequence_number and recomputes SHA-256 hashes in a streaming Python pass -- one DB round-trip regardless of chain length. PostgreSQL triggers on notebook_entries and hash_chains tables raise exceptions on any UPDATE or DELETE attempt. APScheduler executes nightly verification scan for all active notebooks. Nginx handles TLS termination. No public internet exposure.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 (async), asyncpg driver, APScheduler 3.x for nightly scans, hashlib SHA-256 (stdlib). Database: PostgreSQL 16 on EC2 (Docker container). Infrastructure: EC2 t3.medium, Docker Compose, Nginx for TLS. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with pytest and mypy. Monitoring: CloudWatch Agent, structured JSON logs.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Notebook Entry Append: POST /notebook/entries accepts entry content, type, and author; acquires per-notebook advisory lock; computes SHA-256(previous_hash + content + sequence_number); inserts notebook_entries and hash_chains rows atomically; returns entry_id and entry_hash. On-Demand Chain Verification: POST /verification/runs triggers batch SELECT verification and returns run_id; GET /verification/runs/:run_id returns status and tamper event count. Compliance Report Generation: GET /reports/:run_id returns full integrity report with human-readable tamper event descriptions, author identities, timestamps, and ALCOA+-aligned corrective action recommendations. Nightly Automated Scan: APScheduler triggers verification for all notebooks modified in past 24 hours; stores verification_run and integrity_report records. Genesis Entry Handling: sequence_number=0 entries have previous_hash='0'*64 (64-character zero hex string) as defined sentinel value.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI application container: NotebookRouter, VerificationRouter, ReportRouter, HashChainService (SHA-256 append + advisory lock), VerificationService (batch SELECT verification + tamper detection), ReportService (ALCOA+ report generation), APScheduler (nightly scan). PostgreSQL 16 container: tables notebook_entries, hash_chains, tamper_events, verification_runs, integrity_reports; BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE triggers on notebook_entries and hash_chains raising GxP integrity exceptions; application DB user with INSERT + SELECT privileges only on notebook_entries and hash_chains. Nginx container for TLS termination. CloudWatch Agent sidecar for logs and metrics forwarding.
target_output.workflows: Concurrent Notebook Entry Append: two or more clients POST /notebook/entries for the same notebook_id simultaneously; both requests attempt to acquire pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(notebook_id::text)::bigint) within a 5-second timeout; one transaction acquires lock, fetches last sequence_number and entry_hash, computes new SHA-256 hash, inserts both notebook_entries and hash_chains rows, commits and releases lock; second transaction acquires lock after first commits, reads updated last row, computes its hash from first entry as previous_hash -- linear chain maintained with no fork. Batch Chain Verification: POST /verification/runs triggers VerificationService; single SELECT entry_hash, previous_hash, sequence_number FROM hash_chains WHERE notebook_id=:id ORDER BY sequence_number fetched with asyncpg server-side cursor; Python generator iterates rows: for sequence 0 checks previous_hash == '0'*64; for subsequent rows recomputes SHA-256(row.previous_hash + notebook_entries[row.sequence_number].content + str(row.sequence_number)) and compares to stored entry_hash; any mismatch writes tamper_events record with regulatory context fields; verification_runs updated with completed status and tamper event count. ALCOA+ Compliance Report: GET /reports/:run_id joins verification_run, tamper_events, notebook_entries; for each tamper_event generates human-readable regulatory_description ('Entry #[seq] by [author] recorded at [timestamp] as [entry_type]: hash mismatch detected. Expected: [hash prefix]. Stored hash does not match recomputed value, indicating possible retroactive modification.') and corrective_action_recommendation ('Initiate deviation per SOP-QA-012. Quarantine notebook section [seq range]. Determine root cause...').
target_output.data_model: notebook_entries (id UUID PK, notebook_id UUID, sequence_number BIGINT, entry_type ENUM observation/protocol_step/result/correction, content TEXT, author_id VARCHAR(100), created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE, entry_hash VARCHAR(64) -- SHA-256 hex). hash_chains (id UUID PK, notebook_id UUID, sequence_number BIGINT REFERENCES notebook_entries(sequence_number), entry_hash VARCHAR(64), previous_hash VARCHAR(64) -- '0'*64 for genesis, chained_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE). tamper_events (id UUID PK, verification_run_id UUID FK, sequence_number BIGINT, entry_id UUID FK, tamper_type ENUM hash_mismatch/missing_entry/sequence_gap, expected_hash VARCHAR(64), actual_hash VARCHAR(64), detected_at TIMESTAMP, author_id VARCHAR(100), entry_created_at TIMESTAMP, entry_type_label VARCHAR(50), regulatory_description TEXT, corrective_action_recommendation TEXT). verification_runs (id UUID PK, notebook_id UUID, triggered_by VARCHAR, trigger_type ENUM manual/scheduled, started_at TIMESTAMP, completed_at TIMESTAMP, total_entries_checked INT, tamper_events_detected INT, status ENUM running/completed/failed). integrity_reports (id UUID PK, verification_run_id UUID FK, generated_at TIMESTAMP, entries_verified INT, tamper_events_count INT, alcoa_compliance_assessment TEXT).
target_output.api_design: POST /notebook/entries body: {notebook_id, content, entry_type, author_id}. Response 201: {entry_id, sequence_number, entry_hash, chained_at}. Response 409: advisory lock timeout (concurrent write conflict). POST /verification/runs body: {notebook_id}. Response 202: {run_id, status: 'running'}. GET /verification/runs/:run_id. Response 200: {run_id, status, total_entries_checked, tamper_events_detected, duration_seconds}. GET /reports/:run_id. Response 200: full integrity report with alcoa_compliance_assessment, entries_verified, tamper_events array (each with regulatory_description, corrective_action_recommendation, author_id, created_at, entry_type_label). GET /reports/:run_id/tamper-events: paginated tamper events list. GET /health. JWT roles enforced: lab_it_admin on POST endpoints; qa_officer on GET report endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: 21 CFR Part 11 and ALCOA+ integrity controls: PostgreSQL BEFORE UPDATE OR DELETE triggers on notebook_entries and hash_chains raise exception 'GxP data integrity violation: modification of committed ELN records is prohibited' -- tested in pytest with explicit UPDATE attempt. Application DB user granted INSERT + SELECT on notebook_entries and hash_chains; no UPDATE or DELETE grants at DB user level (defense-in-depth beyond trigger). Advisory lock pg_advisory_xact_lock serializes concurrent appends per notebook. JWT authentication with 24-hour expiry; roles: lab_it_admin (write + verify) and qa_officer (read reports). TLS 1.3 via Nginx. Prototype uses synthetic ELN data only per privacy retention policy; no production experiment records in prototype environment.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Docker Compose on EC2 t3.medium: fastapi container (uvicorn), postgres:16 container (persistent volume /var/lib/postgresql/data), nginx container (TLS termination, self-signed cert for prototype). GitHub Actions pipeline: pytest (including concurrent append test, trigger enforcement test, verification performance test) -> mypy strict check -> Docker build -> SSH deploy via docker compose pull && docker compose up -d. No automated rollback mechanism for prototype; manual Docker Compose image rollback via tag. PostgreSQL backups via pg_dump to S3 daily. Database schema managed by Alembic migrations run on container startup.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch Agent on EC2 forwards FastAPI structured JSON logs (endpoint, user_id, notebook_id, operation, duration_ms) to CloudWatch Logs group /eln-prototype/api. Custom CloudWatch metric: verification_run_duration_seconds per notebook_id. APScheduler nightly scan completion logged with entries_checked and tamper_events_detected counts. CloudWatch alarm: verification_run_duration_seconds > 30 seconds for any notebook (early warning before 60-second SLA breach). EC2 host metrics (CPU, memory) via CloudWatch Agent standard config.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EC2 t3.medium: ~$30/month for prototype evaluation period of 6 months. PostgreSQL on same instance -- no RDS cost. S3 daily pg_dump backup: negligible storage cost for prototype data volumes. Total prototype cost: ~$180 over evaluation period. Scaling note: production integration at 500K ELN entries would require PostgreSQL PARTITION BY notebook_id for hash_chains table and parallel verification workers per partition -- out of scope for prototype.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (Round 1): SHA-256 hash chain append for sequential writes, chain verification, PostgreSQL append-only trigger, JWT auth, basic compliance report JSON. Phase 2 (this round): Concurrent append serialization via advisory lock (ISS-001), batch SELECT verification with streaming SHA-256 recomputation (ISS-002), ALCOA+-aligned compliance report with human-readable tamper event descriptions and corrective action recommendations (ISS-003). Phase 3 (roadmap, pending QA evaluation approval): Integration with LabArchives or IDBS E-WorkBook ELN via webhook on entry creation, chain repair procedure for legitimate corrective actions under ALCOA+ (append correction entry referencing original, do not modify original hash), performance testing at 500K entry scale with partitioned PostgreSQL tables.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 type annotations required on all functions; mypy strict mode enforced in CI. SHA-256 computation uses only hashlib stdlib -- no third-party crypto libraries in prototype. HashChainService.append_entry must be the only code path that writes to hash_chains table; direct SQLAlchemy inserts to hash_chains outside this service are prohibited. Pytest suite must include: concurrent 10-writer test per notebook asserting linear chain, UPDATE trigger enforcement test asserting PostgreSQL exception, verification performance test asserting <60s for 10K entry synthetic chain. All compliance report tamper_events must include regulatory_description and corrective_action_recommendation; ReportService raises ValueError if these fields are empty.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Advisory lock timeout under high concurrent write load causing 409 errors for legitimate lab operations. Mitigation: 5-second lock timeout is acceptable for prototype evaluation; production integration would need queue-based serialization for higher write throughput. Risk: Hash chain verification correctness depends on notebook_entries content being immutable -- PostgreSQL trigger covers this but application-level content validation must also prevent null or empty content inserts that would affect hash recomputation. Tradeoff: Synchronous on-demand verification for chains up to 10K entries; larger chains should use async background verification with polling -- prototype implementation defers this to Phase 3.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Genesis entry previous_hash: standardized as 64-character hex string of zeros ('0000...0000') -- consistent across all new notebooks; verified in pytest. Concurrent write handling: PostgreSQL advisory lock chosen over serializable transaction (SELECT FOR UPDATE on last chain entry) because advisory lock avoids row-level lock contention on the hash_chains table under read-heavy verification workloads. Chain repair for legitimate corrections: out of scope for prototype; QA team confirmed ALCOA+ principle requires appending a correction entry referencing the original, never modifying the original -- this will be implemented in Phase 3 production integration.

metadata.notes: Round 2 ELN hash chain integrity prototype: resolved concurrent append race condition (ISS-001), batch verification performance (ISS-002), and ALCOA+-aligned compliance report format (ISS-003). 21 CFR Part 11 and GMP Annex 11 focused.

project_goal.value: Prototype replicating SAS-based NCA and population PK statistical outputs in Python to validate tooling equivalence before full regulatory adoption
project_goal.rationale: Regulatory submissions depend on validated statistical tooling; equivalence evidence is required before switching

target_users.value: Clinical pharmacology analysts and biostatisticians
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of NCA and popPK outputs for IND/NDA submissions

project_class.rationale: Prototype scope with no production SLA; purpose is equivalence validation not feature delivery

capabilities.rationale: Computational and data capabilities required; no UI or auth needed for internal notebook prototype

complexity_level.rationale: PK calculations are numerically complex but workflows are well-defined by pharmacokinetic science

risk_level.rationale: Numerical discrepancies with SAS outputs could affect regulatory conclusions if undetected

data_sensitivity.rationale: Subject-level PK profiles contain individual clinical trial participant data

external_exposure.rationale: Prototype runs on managed Vertex AI Workbench accessible only within corporate GCP project

access_model.value: IAM-gated Vertex AI Workbench with Workload Identity; access restricted to clinical pharmacology team GCP group
access_model.rationale: Prevents unauthorized access to subject-level PK data in managed notebooks

feature_scope.value: NCA parameter computation using PKNCA, popPK model fitting using statsmodels LME, side-by-side equivalence tables versus SAS outputs, versioned notebook snapshots, PostgreSQL results store
feature_scope.rationale: Covers all outputs needed for equivalence validation package

mvp_scope.value: NCA equivalence for single-dose PK studies; store results and SAS reference in PostgreSQL; generate equivalence summary report
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-dose NCA is highest priority for near-term IND submission support

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for all GCP traffic, Cloud KMS CMEK encryption at rest for PostgreSQL and GCS, no data export outside GCP project boundary
security_baseline.rationale: Health data requires encryption at rest and in transit even in prototype context

frontend_stack.value: None; Jupyter notebooks in Vertex AI Workbench are the sole interface
frontend_stack.rationale: Research prototype; no browser UI required

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with PKNCA, statsmodels, scipy, pandas; Jupyter notebooks
backend_stack.rationale: PKNCA is the leading open-source NCA library; statsmodels covers popPK LME needs

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for structured results; GCS bucket for input datasets and notebook snapshots
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for equivalence results; GCS for raw SAS export files and versioned notebooks

hosting_target.value: Vertex AI Workbench managed notebooks on GCP us-central1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed notebook environment with IAM integration and GCP data residency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Subject PK profiles retained for study duration plus 15 years per ICH E9 and FDA regulations; prototype equivalence results retained 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Clinical trial data retention obligations apply even to prototype equivalence studies

future_scope.value: popPK multi-dose equivalence, BE analysis, integration with clinical data management system for automated data pulls
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 scope after MVP equivalence is demonstrated and biostatistics sign-off received

constraints.value: Must use GCP; Python libraries must be open-source or internally licensed; no external API calls for calculations
constraints.rationale: Data residency and IP protection require all computation to stay within GCP project boundary

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring for notebook instance health; Cloud Logging for pipeline execution traces
observability_baseline.rationale: Prototype-level observability; full APM not required

execution_preference.value: Iterative notebook-driven development with versioned snapshots committed to Git after each validation milestone
execution_preference.rationale: Notebook-first approach matches analyst workflow and provides traceable execution history

compliance_context.value: FDA Bioanalytical Method Validation guidance (2018), ICH E9 statistical principles; equivalence report must demonstrate numeric agreement within pre-specified tolerance bands
compliance_context.rationale: Regulatory submission readiness requires documented tooling validation with statistical acceptance criteria

project.goal: Validate Python NCA/popPK equivalence with SAS reference outputs for clinical pharmacology submissions
project.target_users: Clinical pharmacology analysts and biostatisticians

security.auth: IAM + Workload Identity

data.storage: GCS for input datasets and notebook snapshots

constraints.execution: No external API calls for PK calculations

open_questions.tolerance_bands: Acceptance criteria for numeric equivalence not yet formally agreed with biostatistics team

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP focus on single-dose NCA equivalence is well-scoped. PKNCA produces ICH-aligned NCA parameters (AUC_inf, AUC_last, Cmax, Tmax, t1/2, CL/F) directly comparable to SAS PROC PKS outputs. Tolerance bands must be agreed with the biostatistics team before the equivalence report can be accepted for a regulatory package.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Vertex AI Workbench is a sound choice for IAM-gated managed Jupyter with GCP data residency. The PostgreSQL results store enables structured querying of equivalence summaries across multiple study comparisons, which is preferable to ad-hoc CSV comparisons. Parameterizing notebooks with Papermill for Cloud Build non-interactive re-execution addresses reproducibility requirements under ICH E9.
reasoner_reviews.security: Subject-level PK profiles are health data and require Cloud KMS CMEK on Cloud SQL and GCS. Workload Identity prevents service account key sprawl. GCS bucket IAM must restrict access to the clinical pharmacology GCP group only; uniform bucket-level ACL with no allUsers binding.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: GCP-only constraint is met by Vertex AI Workbench plus Cloud SQL and GCS. All PK computation libraries (PKNCA, scipy, statsmodels) run locally in the notebook kernel with no external calls, satisfying the data residency requirement.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The prototype lacks pre-specified numeric tolerance criteria. Without documented acceptance bands (e.g., relative difference below 0.1% for AUC, below 0.5% for Tmax), the equivalence report cannot be used to support a regulatory submission. This gap must be addressed in Phase 1 before first Workbench execution.

backend.service_design: Notebook-based prototype with modular Python src/ package imported by Jupyter notebooks; PKNCA drives NCA parameter calculations, statsmodels LME for popPK nonlinear mixed-effects fitting
backend.business_modules: nca_runner (PKNCA wrapper, AUC/Cmax/t_half extraction per subject), equivalence_checker (tolerance band evaluation, delta computation per parameter), report_generator (HTML and CSV equivalence summary tables), data_loader (Cloud SQL ingestion via psycopg3 from GCS-staged inputs)
backend.failure_handling: Notebook cells catch PKNCA computation failures per subject and log to a failed_subjects table in PostgreSQL with error reason; pipeline continues with remaining subjects rather than aborting
backend.backend_risks: PKNCA and SAS PROC PKS use different default lambda_z estimation algorithms; terminal half-life divergence is expected for sparse sampling designs and must be characterized and documented

security.auth_design: Vertex AI Workbench IAM binding to clinical-pharmacology@ GCP group; Workload Identity Federation for Cloud SQL and GCS access from notebook kernel, no service account key files
security.secrets_management: Cloud Secret Manager for Cloud SQL connection string; no hardcoded credentials in notebooks or committed config files
security.privacy_controls: GCS bucket uniform bucket-level ACL with versioning enabled; Cloud SQL private IP only, no public endpoint; Cloud KMS CMEK for both Cloud SQL and GCS bucket; subject pseudonymization sourced from CDISC ADPC data before ingestion

data.entities: clinical_studies (id, study_id, sponsor_protocol, phase, compound_id, data_loaded_at), subject_profiles (id, study_id, subject_id_blinded, dose_mg, dose_time, sampling_schedule_json), nca_results (id, study_id, subject_id_blinded, tool ENUM python/sas, auc_inf, auc_last, cmax, tmax, t_half, cl_f, vz_f, computed_at), equivalence_reports (id, study_id, parameter, python_mean, sas_mean, relative_diff_pct, within_tolerance BOOLEAN, tolerance_band_pct, generated_at)
data.storage_design: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with CMEK for structured equivalence results; GCS bucket versioned for raw SAS output CSV files and notebook .ipynb snapshots; no local disk persistence on Workbench instance between sessions
data.retention_and_deletion: Clinical study PK data retained 15 years post-study completion; equivalence report artifacts retained 2 years; GCS lifecycle rule deletes temporary staging objects after 30 days

devops.deployment_topology: Vertex AI Workbench managed notebook instance in us-central1; Cloud SQL private IP in same VPC; GCS bucket with VPC Service Controls perimeter; all resources in single GCP project
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build trigger on Git tags matching v{major}.{minor}-{study_id} pattern; executes Papermill to re-run notebooks non-interactively and stores output .ipynb to GCS as validation artifacts
devops.observability_stack: Google Cloud Monitoring for Workbench instance CPU/memory; Cloud Logging for notebook execution traces and Cloud SQL slow query logs; custom log-based metric for PKNCA computation errors per study run
devops.rollback_strategy: GCS-versioned notebook snapshots allow rollback to any prior validated state; Cloud SQL automated backups with 7-day retention for results store recovery

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a clinical pharmacology statistics migration prototype where numeric fidelity between Python PKNCA and legacy SAS PROC PKS outputs is the primary concern. Key decisions: Vertex AI Workbench for IAM-gated managed notebooks with health data residency in GCP, PostgreSQL for structured equivalence result storage enabling cross-study querying, PKNCA as the NCA library. The compliance context (FDA BMV, ICH E9) drives the need for a formal tolerance framework and versioned notebook snapshots as audit artifacts.
target_output.title: SAS-to-Python Clinical PK Statistics Equivalence Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: A GCP-hosted research prototype that reproduces SAS-based NCA and population PK statistical outputs using Python PKNCA and statsmodels, generating side-by-side equivalence tables to validate tooling equivalence before regulatory adoption. Subject-level PK profiles from clinical studies are stored in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with CMEK encryption, computations execute in IAM-gated Vertex AI Workbench managed notebooks, and equivalence reports are versioned in GCS as submission-ready validation artifacts.
target_output.architecture_overview: Notebook-centric prototype architecture on GCP: Vertex AI Workbench managed notebook instances execute Python PK computation modules (PKNCA for NCA, statsmodels LME for popPK) against subject-level datasets ingested from GCS. Results are persisted to Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with CMEK encryption via private-IP Workload Identity connection. Cloud Build triggers Papermill for non-interactive re-execution on Git tags, storing executed notebooks to GCS as versioned validation artifacts. No public endpoints; all access gated by IAM and VPC Service Controls perimeter enclosing health data.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12 runtime in Vertex AI Workbench managed notebooks. PK libraries: PKNCA 0.11 (NCA parameter computation), statsmodels 0.14 (linear mixed-effects popPK), scipy 1.13 (numerical integration fallbacks). Data handling: pandas, pyarrow. Storage: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with Cloud KMS CMEK, GCS with VPC Service Controls. Orchestration: Papermill for parameterized non-interactive notebook execution, Cloud Build for CI triggers. Secrets: Cloud Secret Manager. Monitoring: Google Cloud Monitoring, Cloud Logging.
target_output.functional_feature_map: NCA Runner: accepts NONMEM-format or CDISC ADPC CSV datasets, computes AUC_inf (linear-log trapezoidal), AUC_last, Cmax, Tmax, t1/2, CL/F, Vz/F using PKNCA with configurable lambda_z estimation windows matching SAS settings. Equivalence Checker: joins paired Python and SAS nca_results rows, computes per-parameter relative percent differences, evaluates against pre-specified tolerance bands, flags out-of-tolerance parameters. Report Generator: produces HTML and CSV equivalence summary tables with mean, SD, and 90% CI for relative difference per NCA parameter. Data Loader: ingests SAS dataset CSV exports and ADPC inputs from GCS to PostgreSQL.
target_output.system_components: Vertex AI Workbench managed notebook instance (n1-standard-4, us-central1) for interactive analysis. Cloud Build executor for non-interactive Papermill pipeline reruns triggered by Git tags. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (private IP, CMEK, automated backups, db-g1-small) as results store. GCS bucket with VPC Service Controls for input datasets, SAS reference CSV outputs, and versioned notebook snapshots. Cloud Secret Manager for Cloud SQL credentials. Google Cloud Monitoring and Cloud Logging for instance health and execution observability.
target_output.workflows: NCA Equivalence Run: analyst loads clinical study CDISC ADPC dataset from GCS into pandas DataFrame, calls nca_runner.compute_nca_parameters(df, study_id, lambda_z_config) which invokes PKNCA Concentrations and AUC objects to extract per-subject AUC_inf, Cmax, Tmax, t1/2, CL/F, and writes results to nca_results tagged tool='python'. SAS reference outputs previously exported as CSV are loaded to nca_results tagged tool='sas'. equivalence_checker.evaluate(study_id, tolerance_config) then joins both sets, computes relative_diff_pct per parameter, aggregates to study-level summary, and writes to equivalence_reports. Notebook Versioning: on completion of each equivalence milestone, Cloud Build executes Papermill with injected study_id parameter, stores executed notebook to GCS path /snapshots/{study_id}/{timestamp}.ipynb, and inserts a snapshot_metadata record in PostgreSQL with git_commit_sha and papermill_run_id.
target_output.data_model: clinical_studies (id UUID PK, study_id VARCHAR unique e.g. CPKP-001, sponsor_protocol VARCHAR, phase ENUM phase_1/phase_2/phase_3, compound_id VARCHAR, data_loaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). subject_profiles (id UUID PK, study_id FK, subject_id_blinded VARCHAR, dose_mg NUMERIC, dose_time TIMESTAMPTZ, sampling_schedule_json JSONB). nca_results (id UUID PK, study_id FK, subject_id_blinded VARCHAR, tool ENUM python/sas, auc_inf_hng_ml NUMERIC, auc_last_hng_ml NUMERIC, cmax_ng_ml NUMERIC, tmax_h NUMERIC, t_half_h NUMERIC, cl_f_l_h NUMERIC, vz_f_l NUMERIC, lambda_z_window_json JSONB, computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). equivalence_reports (id UUID PK, study_id FK, parameter VARCHAR, python_mean NUMERIC, sas_mean NUMERIC, relative_diff_pct NUMERIC, within_tolerance BOOLEAN, tolerance_band_pct NUMERIC, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). snapshot_metadata (id UUID PK, study_id FK, gcs_path VARCHAR, git_commit_sha VARCHAR, papermill_run_id VARCHAR, committed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No external REST API. Internal Python module API: nca_runner.compute_nca_parameters(adpc_df: DataFrame, study_id: str, lambda_z_config: dict) -> pd.DataFrame. equivalence_checker.evaluate_study(study_id: str, tolerance_config: dict) -> EquivalenceReport. report_generator.render_html(report: EquivalenceReport, output_path: str) -> None. data_loader.ingest_sas_csv(gcs_uri: str, study_id: str) -> int (rows inserted). Cloud SQL accessed via psycopg3 connection pool initialized from Cloud Secret Manager credentials at notebook startup.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM binding restricts Workbench instance access to clinical-pharmacology@ GCP group. Workload Identity Federation used by notebook kernel for passwordless Cloud SQL and GCS access, eliminating service account key files. Cloud SQL private IP with no public endpoint; VPC Service Controls perimeter prevents data exfiltration beyond project boundary. Cloud KMS CMEK on Cloud SQL and GCS for subject-level PK data at rest. Subject identifiers use pseudonymization sourced from CDISC ADPC blinding before ingestion; no direct study participant IDs in equivalence reports. Compliance: FDA BMV guidance requires documented acceptance criteria in equivalence report; tolerance bands must be specified in a validation plan before any PKNCA execution.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Single prototype environment in GCP project clinical-pharm-prototype (us-central1). Vertex AI Workbench instance, Cloud SQL instance, and GCS bucket all provisioned via Terraform to enable reproducible teardown and re-creation. Cloud Build trigger configured for Git tags v{major}.{minor}-{study_id}; executes Papermill via python -m papermill notebook.ipynb output.ipynb -p study_id {id}, stores output notebook to GCS, and uploads Cloud SQL backup. No multi-environment promotion; prototype is disposable. Cloud SQL automated backups retained 7 days. Secrets rotation enforced manually via Cloud Secret Manager with 90-day policy and rotation reminder alert.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring dashboards track Workbench instance CPU utilization, memory, and disk. Cloud Logging captures Papermill execution trace, individual PKNCA cell stdout, and Cloud SQL slow query log (threshold 500ms). Custom log-based metric counts PKNCA per-subject computation failures per study run; alert fires to clinical-pharm-ops@ if failure rate exceeds 5% in a single run. Cloud Monitoring alert fires if Workbench CPU exceeds 90% for 10 minutes (indicates runaway popPK optimization loop).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $200-350/month for n1-standard-4 Workbench instance (stopped when idle at $0/hr), Cloud SQL db-g1-small (~$50/month), GCS standard storage for 50GB datasets and snapshots (~$1/GB). If equivalence validation expands to 20+ simultaneous studies, Cloud Batch jobs running Papermill on n2-highcpu-8 spot instances at ~$0.28/hour provide parallel execution without persistent Workbench costs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - NCA MVP (6 weeks): provision Workbench and Cloud SQL via Terraform, implement nca_runner and equivalence_checker modules, run equivalence on 3 pilot single-dose CDISC ADPC studies, agree tolerance bands with biostatistics team, generate first equivalence report for regulatory review. Phase 2 - popPK and Automation (6 weeks): implement popPK nonlinear mixed-effects with statsmodels, configure Papermill Cloud Build pipeline for reproducible non-interactive reruns, expand to 10 studies covering single- and multi-dose PK designs, produce full validation package for regulatory submission.
target_output.development_guidelines: Notebook cells must be idempotent: re-execution produces identical results for the same input data version. All PKNCA call parameters (lambda_z_config, AUC method, dose normalization) must be logged to Cloud Logging per run for reproducibility audit. Unit tests for nca_runner and equivalence_checker using pytest with synthetic concentration-time datasets having known ground-truth AUC and Cmax values. nbstripout pre-commit hook strips notebook outputs before Git commit to prevent PHI leakage in repository history. Cloud Build enforces pytest pass gate before storing Papermill execution snapshot.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PKNCA and SAS PROC PKS use different default lambda_z selection algorithms, potentially producing t1/2 differences beyond tolerance for sparse sampling designs; mitigated by exposing lambda_z_config in nca_runner to match SAS LAMBDA settings and documenting residual algorithmic differences in the validation report. Risk: Workbench kernel crash during long popPK LME fits leaves PostgreSQL nca_results in partial state; mitigated by atomic per-subject-batch transactions with rollback on failure. Tradeoff: notebook-first development aids analyst adoption but complicates CI reproducibility; Papermill bridges both requirements. Risk: prototype Cloud SQL db-g1-small storage exhausted by bulk study loads; mitigated by GCS lifecycle archival of processed raw data after 90 days.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected PKNCA over standalone pharmacokinetics PyPI package for richer NCA parameterization and active maintenance. Chose Cloud SQL PostgreSQL over BigQuery for low-latency per-subject queries during interactive analysis. Confirmed Papermill over nbconvert for Cloud Build integration due to parameter injection support. Accepted subject pseudonymization from source CDISC data as sufficient de-identification for prototype; full anonymization deferred to production tooling phase.

metadata.notes: SAS-to-Python NCA/popPK equivalence prototype on GCP with health data and ICH/FDA compliance context.

project_goal.value: CLI tool that parallelizes bulk chemical property calculations (logP, pKa, TPSA) across large compound libraries using multi-process workers, producing structured Parquet output for downstream ADMET modeling pipelines
project_goal.rationale: Informatics teams need reproducible, fast batch calculations replacing ad-hoc notebook cells

target_users.value: Informatics engineers and computational chemists
target_users.rationale: Primary users running large-scale virtual screening property calculations on HPC clusters

project_class.rationale: Command-line interface for HPC SLURM submission; no web UI required

capabilities.rationale: Computation, data transformation, and parallel batch execution are the core needs

complexity_level.rationale: Parallelism strategy and plugin system add complexity beyond simple script

risk_level.rationale: Internal tool operating on internal compound data with no external exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Compound library SMILES are internal IP but not personal or health data

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on HPC nodes with no inbound network ports; output files written to local SLURM scratch or shared filesystem

access_model.value: Unix file system permissions; no application-level auth; SLURM job ownership enforces user isolation
access_model.rationale: HPC cluster user isolation is sufficient for internal-only compound data

feature_scope.value: Multi-process parallel property calculation (logP, pKa, TPSA, MW, HBD, HBA), SDF/SMILES input, Parquet output, YAML-configurable calculator selection, chunk-size tuning, SLURM array job support, progress bar, failed compound error log
feature_scope.rationale: All features needed for production informatics pipeline integration

mvp_scope.value: logP, TPSA, MW via RDKit; multi-process workers; SMILES CSV input; Parquet output; YAML config
mvp_scope.rationale: Core property set needed by 80% of use cases; pKa and plugin system in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: No network access from calculation workers; output files written to user-owned directories; no credentials stored
security_baseline.rationale: Local-only execution requires only filesystem access controls

frontend_stack.value: None; Click CLI with rich progress bars is the sole interface
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool; no browser or desktop UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click and multiprocessing; RDKit for property calculations; optional OpenBabel plugin
backend_stack.rationale: RDKit is the standard cheminformatics toolkit; multiprocessing avoids GIL for CPU-bound calculations

data_platform.value: Parquet files (local or shared NFS); optional DuckDB for in-process aggregation queries
data_platform.rationale: Parquet is the standard columnar format for ADMET pipeline interoperability

hosting_target.value: HPC cluster running SLURM; executed on compute nodes via sbatch
hosting_target.rationale: Informatics compute runs on corporate HPC; no cloud compute needed for this tool

privacy_retention_policy.value: Output Parquet files retained per informatics project policy (typically 2 years); no automated deletion by the tool itself
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Retention managed at project level; CLI output is not a system of record

future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 extensibility requirements identified by informatics team leads

constraints.value: Must run on Python 3.12 on HPC nodes; no conda environments with GUI dependencies; Parquet output must be readable by downstream pandas and Polars pipelines
constraints.rationale: HPC node environment and downstream pipeline compatibility constraints

observability_baseline.value: Rich progress bar with ETA; structured JSON log file per run; failed compound CSV with SMILES and error message
observability_baseline.rationale: Informatics engineers need per-run diagnostics without external monitoring infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Single pip-installable package with CLI entry point; pytest unit tests; GitHub Actions CI for test and build on every PR
execution_preference.rationale: Standard Python packaging for easy HPC deployment via pip install from internal PyPI mirror

project.goal: Parallelized bulk chemical property calculation CLI for HPC SLURM environments
project.target_users: Informatics engineers and computational chemists

security.auth: None; SLURM job ownership

data.storage: Local SLURM scratch or shared NFS

constraints.output: Parquet compatible with pandas and Polars

open_questions.pka_library: Epik license availability for pKa calculations not yet confirmed for HPC nodes

reasoner_reviews.product: Phase 2 adds pKa and plugin support to an MVP that delivered logP, TPSA, and MW. The parallelization strategy using Python multiprocessing with configurable chunk sizes is appropriate for HPC SLURM environments where each node can run 8-64 worker processes. The YAML config approach enables reproducible calculation pipelines logged alongside results.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The current Round 1 plan did not specify a clear plugin interface contract for custom calculators, making third-party property library integration ad-hoc. Round 2 should define a CalculatorPlugin abstract base class with a calculate(smiles: str) -> dict interface so RDKit, OpenBabel, and Epik calculators share a common registration pattern.
reasoner_reviews.security: Local-only execution on HPC with SLURM job isolation has no meaningful network attack surface. The primary risk is SMILES injection into subprocess calls if an external calculator (Epik) is invoked via shell. All external calculator calls must use subprocess with explicit argument lists, never shell=True.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Parquet via pyarrow is confirmed as the output format. DuckDB for in-process aggregation is a lightweight optional dependency. Ensure the package specifies rdkit-pypi as a dependency rather than conda rdkit since HPC nodes use pip-based deployment.

backend.service_design: Click CLI with three commands: calc (run property calculations), validate (check SMILES library for parse errors without calculating), update-db (pull latest filter rule sets from internal registry). Modular calculators registered via entry_points plugin API.
backend.api_patterns: CLI entry points: chemcalc calc --input compounds.smi --output results.parquet --config calc.yaml --workers 16 --chunk-size 1000. chemcalc validate --input compounds.smi --report errors.csv. chemcalc update-db --registry-url http://internal-registry/filters.
backend.business_modules: calculator_registry (CalculatorPlugin ABC, auto-discovers entry_point plugins), worker_pool (multiprocessing Pool with configurable worker count and chunk dispatch), result_assembler (reassembles chunked Parquet results preserving input order), error_logger (per-run failed_compounds CSV and JSON run metadata)
backend.backend_risks: RDKit SMILES parsing returns None for malformed inputs without raising exceptions; worker processes must check for None mol objects before invoking property descriptors to avoid silent null results in output Parquet

data.entities: compound_libraries (input SDF or SMILES files; not persisted, processed in streaming chunks), property_calculations (output Parquet schema: compound_id, smiles, logp_rdkit, tpsa_rdkit, mw_rdkit, hbd, hba, pka_epik NULLABLE, calculator_versions_json, calc_timestamp), calculation_configs (YAML files versioned in Git: calculator list, chunk_size, worker_count, timeout_per_compound_s), output_files (Parquet partitioned by chunk index, merged to single file on completion)
data.schema_notes: Parquet schema uses nullable Float32 for all property values; compound_id is String preserving original input identifier; calculator_versions_json records exact RDKit and Epik versions used for reproducibility
data.retention_and_deletion: Output Parquet files are user-owned on HPC shared filesystem; the CLI does not manage retention; informatics project policy governs file lifecycle

devops.deployment_topology: pip install chemcalc from internal PyPI mirror (devpi) on each HPC compute node; no persistent services; SLURM array jobs invoke chemcalc calc with different input chunk files per array task
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Python 3.12 matrix, pytest with RDKit property ground-truth fixtures, mypy strict type checking, ruff linting, bumpversion on tag push, twine upload to internal devpi registry
devops.observability_stack: Rich progress bar with compound/sec throughput and ETA during interactive runs; JSON run log at {output}.run.json with worker count, chunk size, total compounds, failed count, wall time, RDKit version; failed_compounds.csv with smiles and error_reason columns
devops.devops_risks: RDKit conda vs pip version mismatch on HPC nodes may produce subtly different logP values; pin rdkit-pypi>=2024.3 in requirements and document expected version in run JSON

security.auth_design: No application-level authentication; SLURM job ownership and Unix file permissions provide isolation
security.abuse_prevention: External calculator subprocess calls (Epik) use explicit argument list (subprocess.run([binary, arg1, arg2], shell=False)); input SMILES validated by RDKit mol parse before passing to any external process
security.security_risks: Risk: user-supplied YAML config could specify arbitrary registry URL for update-db; mitigate by allowlisting registry hostname against internal_registry_allowlist in config

ISS-001.title: No plugin interface contract for custom calculators
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described calculator extensibility in prose only; no abstract base class or entry_points registration mechanism was specified, making plugin development inconsistent across teams

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner review

ISS-002.title: Silent null results for malformed SMILES in worker processes
ISS-002.detail: RDKit Chem.MolFromSmiles returns None for invalid SMILES without exception; Round 1 worker implementation did not check for None before computing properties, allowing null rows to appear in output Parquet without appearing in the error log

history.note: Identified during integration testing with ChEMBL extract containing 0.3% malformed SMILES

focus_issues.title: Silent null results for malformed SMILES in worker processes
focus_issues.detail: RDKit returns None for invalid SMILES; workers must validate mol object before property computation and route failures to error log

focus_issues.title: No plugin interface contract for custom calculators
focus_issues.detail: Plugin system needs CalculatorPlugin ABC and entry_points registration to enable consistent third-party integrations

latest_recommendations: Define CalculatorPlugin abstract base class with calculate(smiles: str) -> dict and a name/version property; register via entry_points in pyproject.toml
Add explicit None-check after RDKit MolFromSmiles in every worker cell; route failed compounds to error_logger with SMILES and exception message before continuing
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: ChemCalc Bulk Property Calculation CLI - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues from Round 1; fix_report must address both before re-audit

previous_audits.summary: Sound CLI architecture with good parallelization strategy but two implementation gaps that would cause silent data quality issues in production informatics pipelines.
strengths: Configurable YAML-driven calculator selection enables reproducible pipeline versioning
