multiprocessing Pool with chunk dispatch is correct approach for CPU-bound RDKit work on HPC nodes
Parquet output with calculator version metadata supports downstream reproducibility
concerns: Null mol handling not addressed in worker code; malformed SMILES produce silent nulls in output
Plugin extensibility described in prose only; no formal interface contract
blocking_issues: ISS-002 causes data quality corruption in output Parquet files that downstream ADMET models would ingest without warning
recommendations: Add None guard after every RDKit MolFromSmiles call; route to error_logger with compound_id, smiles, and error_reason
Define CalculatorPlugin(ABC) with abstract calculate(smiles: str) -> dict[str, float] and name: str property; auto-discover via importlib.metadata entry_points group 'chemcalc.calculators'

issue_updates.title: Silent null results for malformed SMILES
issue_updates.detail: Worker implementation lacks None check; null rows observed in pilot Parquet output for 0.3% of ChEMBL SMILES

issue_updates.title: No plugin interface contract
issue_updates.detail: Plugin extensibility described in plan text but not specified as code interface

previous_plan.title: ChemCalc Bulk Property Calculation CLI - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Python CLI tool using Click and multiprocessing to parallelize RDKit-based logP, TPSA, and MW calculations across compound library SMILES files, writing Parquet output for downstream ADMET modeling pipelines on HPC SLURM clusters.
previous_plan.system_components: Click CLI entry point, multiprocessing Pool dispatcher, RDKit property calculation workers, pyarrow Parquet writer, YAML config loader, Rich progress bar. No persistent services.
previous_plan.workflows: User runs chemcalc calc --input library.smi --output results.parquet --workers 16; CLI splits input into 1000-compound chunks, dispatches to Pool, workers compute RDKit descriptors, results assembled and written to Parquet.
previous_plan.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: malformed SMILES cause worker exceptions and partial output. Risk: RDKit version differences between dev and HPC produce divergent logP values. Mitigations not yet fully implemented.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision must address ISS-002 (silent null SMILES handling) as the high-severity blocking issue and ISS-001 (plugin interface contract) as the medium-severity architectural gap. The fix for ISS-002 is a None guard in every worker function with routing to error_logger. The fix for ISS-001 is defining a CalculatorPlugin ABC and entry_points registration. The rest of the plan builds cleanly on the Round 1 CLI architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit None guard after every RDKit Chem.MolFromSmiles call in worker functions; failed compounds are routed to error_logger.record_failure(compound_id, smiles, reason) which appends to failed_compounds.csv and the JSON run log; None mol compounds are never written to output Parquet
fix_report.expected_outcome: Output Parquet contains no silent null rows; all malformed SMILES appear in failed_compounds.csv with error reason; downstream ADMET models receive only valid property rows

fix_report.action_taken: Defined CalculatorPlugin abstract base class with abstract methods calculate(smiles: str, mol: Chem.Mol) -> dict[str, float] and properties name: str, version: str; RDKit and OpenBabel implementations subclass it; plugins auto-discovered via importlib.metadata entry_points group 'chemcalc.calculators'; YAML config references plugin names by the name property
fix_report.expected_outcome: Third-party calculator authors implement CalculatorPlugin and register via pyproject.toml entry_points; calc command discovers and invokes all registered plugins uniformly

target_output.title: ChemCalc Bulk Property Calculation CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 CLI tool using Click and multiprocessing that parallelizes bulk chemical property calculations (logP, TPSA, MW, HBD, HBA, pKa) across compound library SMILES files on HPC SLURM clusters, writing structured Parquet output with per-run reproducibility metadata for downstream ADMET modeling pipelines. Round 2 resolves silent null handling for malformed SMILES and introduces a formal CalculatorPlugin entry_points interface for extensible third-party property library integration.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-binary Python CLI installable via pip from internal devpi registry. Click commands (calc, validate, update-db) serve as entry points. The calc command loads a YAML config specifying which CalculatorPlugin implementations to run, splits the input SMILES/SDF file into configurable chunks, and dispatches chunks to a multiprocessing.Pool. Each worker process instantiates the calculator registry, validates mol objects after RDKit parse, computes properties for valid compounds, and routes None mol results to the error_logger. Assembled Parquet files are written per chunk then merged. No network access during calculation; no persistent services.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12. CLI: Click 8.1 with rich 13 for progress bars. Cheminformatics: rdkit-pypi 2024.3 (logP, TPSA, MW, HBD, HBA via Descriptors module), OpenBabel 3.1 (optional alternate logP). Parallelism: multiprocessing.Pool with spawn context for RDKit fork safety. Data: pyarrow 16 for Parquet read/write, DuckDB 0.10 optional for post-processing aggregation. Config: PyYAML 6.0. Packaging: pyproject.toml with entry_points for plugin registration, twine for internal devpi upload. CI: GitHub Actions with pytest, ruff, mypy strict.
target_output.functional_feature_map: calc command: loads CalculatorPlugin registry from entry_points, validates input SMILES/SDF, dispatches chunks to Pool workers, reassembles output Parquet with compound_id, smiles, per-property columns, calculator_versions_json, calc_timestamp. validate command: dry-runs RDKit mol parse across entire input file, outputs failed_compounds.csv without computing properties, useful for pre-flight checks on large libraries. update-db command: fetches latest PAINS/restricted-reagent filter SQLite database from internal registry URL (allowlisted), verifies SHA256 checksum, replaces local copy. Error handling: all None mol results routed to failed_compounds.csv with compound_id, original_smiles, error_reason; run summary written to {output}.run.json.
target_output.system_components: Click CLI entry point (chemcalc). CalculatorPlugin ABC with abstract calculate(smiles, mol) -> dict[str, float] and name/version properties. RDKitCalculator (logP via Crippen, TPSA via rdMolDescriptors, MW, HBD, HBA), OpenBabelCalculator (optional). calculator_registry: auto-discovers plugins via importlib.metadata.entry_points(group='chemcalc.calculators'). worker_pool: multiprocessing.Pool with spawn, configurable worker count and per-compound timeout via multiprocessing.pool.AsyncResult.get(timeout). result_assembler: sorts chunk Parquet files by chunk index, concatenates with pyarrow.concat_tables. error_logger: thread-safe CSV appender for failed_compounds, JSON run metadata writer.
target_output.workflows: Bulk Calculation Run: user invokes chemcalc calc --input library.smi --output results.parquet --config calc.yaml --workers 16 --chunk-size 2000; CLI reads YAML, discovers registered calculators, validates that all named calculators are available in registry (fails fast otherwise), splits input into 2000-compound chunks, dispatches via Pool.imap_unordered; each worker iterates compounds, calls Chem.MolFromSmiles, checks mol is not None (routes failure to error queue), calls each CalculatorPlugin.calculate(smiles, mol), accumulates rows as dicts, writes chunk Parquet; Rich progress bar updates on chunk completion; result_assembler merges chunk files and writes final results.parquet and results.run.json. SLURM Array Run: SLURM array job splits input file into N array tasks, each invoking chemcalc calc with a pre-split chunk file; final results assembled by a follow-up SLURM step invoking chemcalc merge.
target_output.data_model: Output Parquet schema (pyarrow): compound_id String, smiles String, logp_rdkit Float32 nullable, tpsa_rdkit Float32 nullable, mw_rdkit Float32 nullable, hbd_rdkit Int16 nullable, hba_rdkit Int16 nullable, pka_epik Float32 nullable (if Epik plugin registered), calculator_versions_json String (JSON dict of plugin name to version), calc_timestamp Timestamp(us, UTC). compound_libraries: input SDF or SMILES files, not persisted. calculation_configs: YAML files versioned in Git, schema: {calculators: [rdkit, openbabel], chunk_size: 2000, worker_count: 16, timeout_per_compound_s: 5}. output_files: Parquet plus {output}.run.json and {output}.failed.csv.
target_output.api_design: CLI command surface: chemcalc calc [OPTIONS] (--input PATH required, --output PATH required, --config PATH default calc.yaml, --workers INT default cpu_count(), --chunk-size INT default 1000). chemcalc validate [OPTIONS] (--input PATH required, --report PATH default failed_compounds.csv). chemcalc update-db [OPTIONS] (--registry-url URL, --checksum SHA256). Plugin API: class CalculatorPlugin(ABC): @abstractmethod def calculate(self, smiles: str, mol: 'Chem.Mol') -> dict[str, float]: ... @property @abstractmethod def name(self) -> str: ... @property @abstractmethod def version(self) -> str: ... Plugin registration in pyproject.toml: [project.entry-points.'chemcalc.calculators'] rdkit = 'chemcalc.calculators.rdkit:RDKitCalculator'.
target_output.security_and_compliance: No network access during calc or validate commands. update-db command restricts registry URL to internal_registry_allowlist defined in user config (~/.chemcalc/config.toml) to prevent arbitrary URL fetch. Epik external calculator invoked via subprocess.run([epik_binary, '--smiles', smiles, '--out', tmp_file], shell=False, timeout=timeout_per_compound_s) with explicit arg list, never shell=True. Output Parquet files inherit Unix permissions of calling user; no world-readable permissions applied by the tool. No compound SMILES logged to JSON run log; only compound count and failure count.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Package distributed via internal devpi PyPI mirror as chemcalc pip-installable wheel. HPC node setup: module load python/3.12, pip install chemcalc==2.0.0 --index-url http://internal-devpi/simple. SLURM job template: #SBATCH --array=0-99 with chemcalc calc invoked per array task against pre-split input chunks. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: pytest (unit + integration with synthetic SMILES fixtures), mypy strict, ruff lint, bumpversion on release tag, twine upload to devpi. No persistent deployment; each SLURM job is a fresh invocation.
target_output.observability: Rich progress bar shows compounds/sec, ETA, worker count, and chunk completion count during interactive runs. {output}.run.json records: tool_version, rdkit_version, plugin_versions_dict, input_file, total_compounds, successful_count, failed_count, worker_count, chunk_size, wall_time_s, started_at, completed_at. {output}.failed.csv records: compound_id, original_smiles, error_reason (e.g. 'rdkit_parse_none', 'worker_timeout', 'epik_subprocess_error'). --verbose flag enables per-chunk DEBUG log to stderr via Python logging.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud cost; runs on existing HPC SLURM allocation. Throughput benchmark on 32-core node: ~12,000 compounds/sec for RDKit-only logP+TPSA+MW. For 10M compound library: ~14 minutes on 32-core node, or ~1.5 minutes with 10-node SLURM array. Memory: 32-worker process pool with 2000-compound chunks requires ~1.5GB RSS on 32-core node. SLURM array parallelism scales linearly.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (done): logP, TPSA, MW via RDKit, multi-process Pool, SMILES CSV input, Parquet output, YAML config. Phase 2 (current, 4 weeks): ISS-002 None guard fix, ISS-001 CalculatorPlugin ABC and entry_points system, OpenBabel alternate logP plugin, chemcalc merge command for SLURM array assembly, chemcalc validate dry-run command, update-db with checksum verification. Phase 3 (6 weeks): Epik pKa plugin (pending license), AWS Batch cloud-burst mode for >10M compound libraries, DuckDB in-process aggregation for post-run statistics.
target_output.development_guidelines: All worker functions must check 'if mol is None: error_logger.record_failure(cid, smi, reason); continue' before any RDKit descriptor call. CalculatorPlugin subclasses must not import from each other; each plugin is independently loadable. pytest fixtures provide 10 known SMILES with ground-truth RDKit logP/TPSA values for regression tests; CI fails if any output differs by >0.001. ruff enforces no-shell-True rule for subprocess calls. mypy strict type checking with rdkit-stubs for type safety. All new calculators must include a version property returning the underlying library version string.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: RDKit MolFromSmiles returns None for SMILES that are valid by other toolkits (e.g., wildcard atoms); mitigated by logging all None cases with original SMILES so analysts can triage. Risk: multiprocessing spawn context on some HPC nodes may have slow startup overhead for many small jobs; mitigated by minimum chunk-size of 500 and Pool reuse across chunks. Risk: SLURM array task failures leave partial chunk Parquets; chemcalc merge performs chunk completeness check before assembly. Tradeoff: plugin ABC over simple dict-of-functions trades startup overhead for extensibility required by Phase 3 Epik integration.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose multiprocessing over concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor for explicit control of worker Pool lifecycle and imap_unordered ordering. Selected pyarrow over pandas for Parquet writing to avoid pandas dtype inference on nullable float columns. Confirmed entry_points plugin mechanism over importlib.util.spec_from_file_location to support standard pip-installable third-party plugins. Deferred Epik pKa to Phase 3 pending license procurement.

metadata.notes: Cheminformatics bulk property CLI on HPC SLURM; Round 2 fixes silent null SMILES handling and adds CalculatorPlugin ABC.

profile.domain: Federated Learning for Cross-Pharma Toxicity Prediction

project_goal.value: Research prototype evaluating federated learning approaches to train toxicity prediction models across multiple pharma company data silos without sharing raw compound structures or assay data
project_goal.rationale: Individual company datasets for rare toxicity endpoints are too small for robust model training; federation enables collaboration without IP disclosure

target_users.value: Computational toxicologists and ML engineers at participating pharma partner organizations
target_users.rationale: The prototype must be deployable at each partner site by their ML engineering team without vendor support

project_class.rationale: Prototype scope to evaluate federated learning viability; no production SLA or multi-tenant deployment yet

capabilities.rationale: Model training, gradient aggregation, and performance analytics; no browser UI needed for prototype

complexity_level.rationale: Federated learning coordination, mTLS inter-node communication, differential privacy noise injection, and cross-company deployment complexity

risk_level.rationale: Gradient updates from partner nodes could leak proprietary compound structure information via model inversion attacks

data_sensitivity.rationale: Compound toxicity assay data represents proprietary drug discovery IP; even gradient updates carry structural information

external_exposure.rationale: Central aggregator communicates with partner nodes over mTLS; partner-facing API not accessible to public internet

access_model.value: mTLS client certificates for all aggregator-to-node communication; Azure AD Managed Identity for internal Azure resource access; no username/password authentication
access_model.rationale: mTLS provides mutual authentication between aggregator and partner nodes without shared secrets

feature_scope.value: FedAvg gradient aggregation, local model training on partner toxicity datasets, mTLS communication, differential privacy noise injection on gradients, global model evaluation on Tox21 benchmark, round-by-round performance logging, participant node management
feature_scope.rationale: Covers the full federated learning evaluation including privacy-preserving gradient sharing

mvp_scope.value: FedAvg aggregation with 2-3 simulated partner nodes, local PyTorch GCN training on hERG toxicity endpoint, global model Tox21 evaluation, mTLS authentication, round performance logging to PostgreSQL
mvp_scope.rationale: hERG is the highest-priority toxicity endpoint; 2-3 simulated nodes validate the federated protocol before live partner deployment

security_baseline.value: mTLS with 2048-bit certificates for all aggregator-node gRPC channels, differential privacy Gaussian noise on gradient updates (epsilon=1.0, delta=1e-5), no raw compound SMILES transmitted between nodes
security_baseline.rationale: mTLS prevents man-in-the-middle; DP noise limits gradient-based model inversion attacks on partner compound data

frontend_stack.value: None; prototype uses command-line scripts and Jupyter notebooks for analysis
frontend_stack.rationale: Research prototype; no production UI required

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Flower federated learning framework; PyTorch 2 for local model training; gRPC for aggregator-node communication
backend_stack.rationale: Flower provides the federated learning coordination protocol; PyTorch for GCN model; gRPC for efficient binary gradient transmission

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 at aggregator for round performance metrics; local partner data remains on-premise at each partner node, never transmitted
data_platform.rationale: Aggregator stores only global model metrics and round statistics; no compound data ever reaches the aggregator

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Instances for aggregator; partner nodes run in each company's own Azure subscription via Flower client container
hosting_target.rationale: ACI for aggregator matches Azure-first constraint; partner nodes deploy independently in their own infrastructure

privacy_retention_policy.value: Aggregator retains global model artifacts and round metrics for 2 years; partner node local data governed by each company's own data policy; gradient updates not persisted after aggregation
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Gradient updates are transient; only aggregated model weights are retained as research artifacts

future_scope.value: Live partner node deployment across 3-5 pharma companies, additional toxicity endpoints (Ames, DILI, CYP inhibition), secure aggregation (cryptographic gradient protection), production federated platform with web management UI
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 extends to production multi-partner deployment if prototype demonstrates model quality improvement

constraints.value: Must use Azure; partner nodes must be deployable in any Azure region by partner teams without aggregator vendor access; no raw compound data crosses company boundaries
constraints.rationale: Cross-company data sharing agreements prohibit any raw compound structure transmission; Azure aligns with corporate platform standard

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor for aggregator container health; PostgreSQL round metrics table for per-round global model performance; partner node local logs never shipped to aggregator
observability_baseline.rationale: Aggregator observability only; partner node telemetry stays within each company's own monitoring infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Research iteration cycles with documented round results; code versioned in shared Git repo; Docker images published to Azure Container Registry
execution_preference.rationale: Reproducibility and partner deployment require versioned Docker images and Git-tracked experiment configurations

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 25 data minimization for cross-border EU partner data; inter-company data sharing agreements prohibiting raw compound transmission; differential privacy provides technical safeguard against gradient inversion
compliance_context.rationale: EU partner data requires GDPR-compliant processing; DP noise satisfies technical control requirement for cross-border gradient sharing

project.goal: Evaluate federated learning for cross-pharma toxicity model training without raw data sharing
project.target_users: Computational toxicologists and ML engineers at partner pharma companies

backend.framework: Flower federated learning framework, PyTorch 2, gRPC
backend.api: Flower gRPC protocol for aggregator-node communication

security.auth: mTLS client certificates
security.compliance: GDPR data minimization, inter-company data sharing agreements

constraints.data_sharing: No raw compound structures or assay data transmitted between nodes

open_questions.dp_epsilon: Optimal differential privacy epsilon budget for hERG gradient updates not yet established; tradeoff with model accuracy needs empirical evaluation

confirmed_decisions.model_arch: PyTorch GCN (graph convolutional network) for molecular graph representation

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP focus on hERG toxicity with 2-3 simulated partner nodes is the right approach for validating the federated protocol before live multi-company deployment. FedAvg is the correct starting aggregation algorithm; FedProx or SCAFFOLD can be evaluated in later rounds if data heterogeneity (non-IID compound distributions across partners) causes convergence issues.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Flower's built-in gRPC server handles aggregator-node communication well. The key architectural concern is the mTLS certificate management: each partner node needs a unique client certificate, and the aggregator needs a certificate authority to validate them. Azure Key Vault should serve as the certificate store for the aggregator side; partner certificate issuance procedure must be documented for the partner onboarding guide.
reasoner_reviews.security: Differential privacy Gaussian noise at epsilon=1.0 is a reasonable starting point for gradient protection but the tradeoff with model accuracy for small hERG datasets (typically 1,000-5,000 compounds per partner) needs empirical measurement. Model inversion attack evaluation against the aggregated gradients should be included as a prototype evaluation milestone.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Azure Container Instances is suitable for the prototype aggregator given its stateless invocation pattern per federation round. PostgreSQL on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server handles round metrics with no availability concerns at prototype scale. Partner node containers must be tested for Azure subscription isolation to confirm no cross-subscription network visibility.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The prototype lacks an explicit partner onboarding and certificate revocation procedure. Without a defined process for revoking a compromised partner node certificate, a malicious or compromised partner could continue submitting gradient updates indefinitely. This must be addressed in the security design even at prototype stage.

backend.service_design: Flower server (aggregator) runs as ACI container; Flower client (partner node) runs as Docker container at each partner site. FedAvg strategy implemented as custom Flower Strategy subclass with differential privacy noise injection in aggregate_fit.
backend.business_modules: fl_aggregator (Flower Strategy subclass: receives gradient updates, applies FedAvg, injects Gaussian DP noise at aggregate level, stores round metrics), fl_client (Flower NumPyClient: loads local hERG dataset, trains PyTorch GCN for N local epochs, returns gradient update), model_evaluator (evaluates global model on Tox21 hERG benchmark after each round), certificate_manager (loads mTLS certs from Azure Key Vault for aggregator; partner nodes load from local cert store)
backend.failure_handling: Flower server tolerates partial round completion: if min_available_clients of 2 is met from N registered clients, round proceeds. Dropped partner connections are logged to federation_rounds table. Aggregator retries failed rounds up to 3 times before marking round as failed.
backend.backend_risks: Non-IID compound distributions across partner nodes may cause FedAvg to diverge or produce biased global model favoring compounds in largest partner dataset; monitor per-class AUC across rounds to detect bias

security.auth_design: mTLS on all Flower gRPC channels: aggregator presents server certificate issued by internal CA; each partner node presents a unique client certificate also issued by internal CA. Certificate CN encodes partner_id for audit logging.
security.secrets_management: Aggregator server certificate and CA key stored in Azure Key Vault; loaded into Flower server at startup via azure-keyvault-secrets SDK. Partner node client certificates distributed out-of-band via secure partner onboarding package; stored in each partner's local cert store.
security.abuse_prevention: Aggregator validates partner certificate CN against approved_partners list in PostgreSQL before accepting gradient update. Certificate revocation: remove partner from approved_partners and issue CRL; CRL checked at each round startup. Gradient update size bounds enforced: updates with L2-norm > 3 standard deviations from round mean are rejected as potential poisoning attacks.
security.privacy_controls: Gaussian differential privacy noise applied to aggregated gradient with epsilon=1.0, delta=1e-5 using PyTorch Opacus GradSampleModule. No raw SMILES or fingerprint vectors transmitted; only gradient weight tensors exchanged via gRPC.

data.entities: PostgreSQL aggregator: federation_rounds (id, round_number, started_at, completed_at, participating_nodes INT, global_model_auc FLOAT, global_model_loss FLOAT, dp_epsilon_applied FLOAT), participating_nodes (id, partner_id VARCHAR, certificate_cn VARCHAR, last_seen_round INT, status ENUM active/revoked), global_model_artifacts (id, round_id FK, model_state_dict_gcs_path, checkpoint_hash SHA256, created_at). Local partner data (never transmitted): herg_assay_data (compound_smiles, label ENUM active/inactive, assay_protocol, source_dataset).
data.storage_design: Aggregator: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server for round metrics; Azure Blob Storage for global model checkpoint artifacts. Partner nodes: local SQLite or CSV for compound assay data; never connected to aggregator database.
data.analytics_events: Per-round metrics logged to federation_rounds: participating node count, global AUC on Tox21 hERG benchmark (public data, safe to log at aggregator), mean loss, DP epsilon consumed. Partner node local AUC on held-out partner data logged locally only, not transmitted.

devops.deployment_topology: Aggregator: Azure Container Instance running fl-aggregator:v{tag} image pulled from Azure Container Registry; exposes gRPC port 50051 with TLS. Partner node: Docker container fl-client:v{tag} deployed in partner's own Azure subscription; connects to aggregator gRPC endpoint with mTLS client cert.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: pytest for FedAvg aggregation unit tests, mTLS handshake integration test, DP noise calibration test, Docker build and push to ACR on Git tag. Partner deployment: Docker pull command and cert installation script documented in partner onboarding guide.
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor container metrics for aggregator ACI (CPU, memory). PostgreSQL federation_rounds table as primary observability artifact. Azure Monitor alert if aggregator ACI exits unexpectedly. Partner node logs remain local; no log shipping to aggregator.
devops.devops_risks: ACI stateless restart reloads mTLS certificates from Key Vault on each start; if Key Vault throttles during many rapid restarts, aggregator startup fails; mitigate with retry logic in certificate_manager startup code

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a federated learning prototype with partner_facing exposure and confidential IP data, requiring mTLS for all inter-node communication and differential privacy on gradient updates. Key decisions: Flower for FL coordination, PyTorch GCN for molecular graph representation of compounds, Azure Container Instances for the aggregator, PostgreSQL at aggregator only (never partner data), Azure Key Vault for certificate management. The GDPR and inter-company data sharing compliance context drives the no-raw-data constraint and DP noise requirement.
target_output.title: Federated Toxicity Prediction Prototype - Cross-Pharma FL Evaluation
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype on Azure implementing federated learning across simulated pharma partner nodes to train a hERG toxicity prediction GCN model without sharing raw compound structures. The Flower-based aggregator runs in Azure Container Instances, uses FedAvg with Gaussian differential privacy noise on aggregated gradients, and communicates with partner nodes via mTLS gRPC channels. Global model performance is evaluated on the public Tox21 hERG benchmark after each round; round metrics and model artifacts are stored at the aggregator in PostgreSQL and Azure Blob Storage. No compound SMILES or assay data ever leaves partner boundaries.
target_output.architecture_overview: Hub-and-spoke federated architecture: a central Flower aggregator in Azure Container Instances coordinates federation rounds with partner Flower client containers running in each company's own Azure subscription. Communication uses gRPC with mTLS mutual authentication (unique per-partner client certificates validated against approved_partners list). FedAvg aggregation applies Gaussian DP noise (epsilon=1.0) to the aggregated gradient before global model update. Each partner node trains a PyTorch GCN on its local hERG assay dataset for a fixed number of local epochs, returns gradient weight updates (not raw data) to the aggregator, and receives the updated global model. Aggregator PostgreSQL stores only round performance metrics and model artifact references; no partner compound data reaches the aggregator.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12. Federated learning: Flower 1.8 (gRPC server/client). Model: PyTorch 2.3, PyTorch Geometric 2.5 for GCN layers, RDKit 2024.3 for SMILES-to-molecular-graph conversion. Differential privacy: PyTorch Opacus for Gaussian noise injection on gradients. Communication: gRPC with grpc-tools, mTLS via Python ssl module and Azure Key Vault. Databases: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (aggregator metrics). Storage: Azure Blob Storage for model checkpoints. Infrastructure: Azure Container Instances, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault, Azure Monitor. CI: Azure DevOps Pipelines. Benchmark data: Tox21 hERG from DeepChem datasets.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Federation Round Coordinator: Flower server implements custom FedAvgWithDP strategy; broadcasts global model to available partner clients at round start; collects gradient updates; applies FedAvg; injects Gaussian noise; updates global model; evaluates on Tox21 hERG benchmark; logs round metrics to PostgreSQL. Partner Node Client: Flower NumPyClient loads local hERG assay data; converts SMILES to molecular graphs via RDKit; trains PyTorch GCN for 5 local epochs; returns gradient update as NumPy arrays via gRPC. Certificate Manager: loads aggregator server cert from Azure Key Vault; validates partner client certificate CN against approved_partners on each connection. Model Evaluation: after each aggregation round, evaluates global model on Tox21 hERG held-out test set, computing AUC-ROC, AP, and accuracy; results stored in federation_rounds.
target_output.system_components: Flower gRPC Server (aggregator, ACI container, port 50051 mTLS): FedAvgWithDP strategy, certificate validation, round state management. Flower gRPC Client (partner node, local Docker container): GCNTrainer, local dataset loader, gradient preparation. GCNTrainer: PyTorch Geometric GCN with 3 message-passing layers, global mean pooling, binary classification head for hERG active/inactive. DPNoiseInjector: PyTorch Opacus GradSampleModule wrapping aggregated gradients; Gaussian noise at epsilon=1.0, delta=1e-5. CertificateManager: Azure Key Vault SDK for aggregator cert; approved_partners table in PostgreSQL for CN validation. ModelEvaluator: Tox21 hERG benchmark loader from DeepChem; AUC-ROC computation per round. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (federation_rounds, participating_nodes, global_model_artifacts). Azure Blob Storage (model checkpoint state_dicts). Azure Container Registry (fl-aggregator and fl-client versioned images).
target_output.workflows: Federation Round Execution: aggregator Flower server waits for min_available_clients=2 partner connections on gRPC port 50051; validates each connecting client certificate CN against approved_partners; broadcasts current global model weights as NumPy arrays; each partner client receives global model, loads into local PyTorch GCN, trains on local hERG dataset for 5 epochs with Adam optimizer (lr=0.001), computes gradient delta (trained_weights - global_weights), returns gradient as NumPy arrays via gRPC; aggregator computes FedAvg weighted by partner dataset size; DPNoiseInjector applies Gaussian noise to aggregated gradient; global model updated; ModelEvaluator computes Tox21 AUC-ROC; federation_rounds row inserted with participating_nodes count, global AUC, loss, dp_epsilon_applied; round repeated for N_rounds=50. Partner Onboarding: aggregator admin generates unique client certificate for partner (signed by internal CA); certificate CN set to partner_id; participating_nodes row inserted with status=active; partner receives Docker pull command for fl-client image and cert installation script; partner deploys container in their Azure subscription and connects to aggregator gRPC endpoint.
target_output.data_model: PostgreSQL aggregator: federation_rounds (id UUID PK, round_number INT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, participating_nodes INT, global_model_auc_roc FLOAT, global_model_avg_precision FLOAT, global_model_loss FLOAT, dp_epsilon_applied FLOAT, fedavg_weights_json JSONB). participating_nodes (id UUID PK, partner_id VARCHAR unique, certificate_cn VARCHAR, onboarded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_seen_round INT, status ENUM active/revoked, revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ). global_model_artifacts (id UUID PK, round_id FK, blob_storage_path VARCHAR, checkpoint_hash_sha256 VARCHAR, model_arch_version VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Local partner data (never transmitted to aggregator): herg_compounds (smiles VARCHAR, label TINYINT 0/1, assay_protocol VARCHAR, source_dataset VARCHAR); stored as CSV or SQLite at partner site.
target_output.api_design: No REST API. Flower gRPC protocol surfaces: aggregator gRPC endpoint fl.aggregator.internal:50051 (mTLS required). Flower server methods called by client: get_parameters() -> model weights NumPy, fit(parameters, config) -> updated weights NumPy + metrics dict, evaluate(parameters, config) -> loss FLOAT + metrics dict. Aggregator admin CLI: python fl_admin.py add-partner --partner-id PHARMA_B --cert-cn pharma-b-node-1 (inserts participating_nodes row). python fl_admin.py revoke-partner --partner-id PHARMA_B (updates status=revoked, adds CN to CRL). python fl_admin.py run-federation --rounds 50 --min-clients 2 (launches Flower server).
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS on all Flower gRPC channels: aggregator presents certificate signed by internal CA; partner clients present unique certificates with CN=partner_id; aggregator validates client cert CN against approved_partners before accepting any gradient update. Certificate revocation: remove from approved_partners and regenerate CRL; CRL reloaded at each round startup. Gradient poisoning defense: gradient L2-norm computed per partner; updates with norm > mean + 3*std rejected with participating_node alert. Differential privacy: Gaussian noise with epsilon=1.0, delta=1e-5 applied to aggregated gradient using Opacus; DP budget tracked per round in federation_rounds. No raw compound SMILES transmitted: only PyTorch gradient NumPy arrays cross company boundaries; gRPC binary format provides no human-readable compound data. Azure Key Vault for aggregator certificate storage. GDPR compliance: no personal data processed; compound toxicity data is not personal data under GDPR Article 4(1); gradient updates treated as confidential IP under inter-company data sharing agreements.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Aggregator deployed as Azure Container Instance in resource group fl-prototype-rg (East US). ACI started on-demand per federation session via az container create with gRPC port 50051 exposed via internal VNET; not exposed to public internet. Azure Container Registry stores fl-aggregator:v{tag} and fl-client:v{tag} images built by Azure DevOps Pipelines on Git tags. Partner nodes: partner teams run docker pull {acr}/fl-client:v{tag} in their own Azure subscription; cert installation script provided in onboarding package. Azure DevOps Pipeline: pytest unit tests (FedAvg aggregation, DP noise calibration, mTLS handshake mock), Docker build and push to ACR, integration test with 2 simulated partner containers on CI runner. No persistent aggregator service between federation sessions; ACI deleted after each experiment run.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor container metrics for aggregator ACI: CPU, memory, network. PostgreSQL federation_rounds table as the primary observability record: per-round global AUC-ROC, loss, participating_nodes count, DP epsilon consumed, round duration. Python logging in fl_aggregator to stdout (captured by ACI log stream): connection events, certificate validation failures, gradient norm outlier rejections, round completion events. Azure Monitor alert on unexpected ACI container exit. No observability data transmitted from partner nodes to aggregator; partner node stdout logs captured locally by partner's own infrastructure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $50-100/month for prototype: ACI on-demand per session (~$0.0012/vCPU-second, 4 vCPU = ~$1.20/hour per run), Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server Burstable B1ms (~$15/month), Azure Blob Storage for model checkpoints (~$1/month for 50 round checkpoints at 10MB each), Azure Container Registry Basic (~$5/month). Federation round runtime: 50 rounds x 3 minutes/round (local training + gRPC transfer) = ~2.5 hours/session. Scaling to 10 partner nodes requires no aggregator infrastructure change; Flower scales to 100s of clients on ACI with minimal CPU increase.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - Simulated FL Prototype (8 weeks): aggregator and partner node containers, FedAvg with DP noise, mTLS authentication, 2-3 simulated partner nodes (same company, partitioned hERG dataset), Tox21 benchmark evaluation, PostgreSQL round metrics. Phase 2 - Privacy Evaluation (4 weeks): model inversion attack simulation against aggregated gradients, DP epsilon sensitivity analysis (epsilon 0.1-10 vs AUC-ROC), gradient poisoning defense validation, non-IID compound distribution impact assessment. Phase 3 - Live Partner Deployment (4 weeks): onboard 1-2 real pharma partner nodes with live hERG assay data, partner onboarding runbook, certificate lifecycle procedures, inter-company data sharing agreement review.
target_output.development_guidelines: FedAvgWithDP strategy must include unit tests verifying: gradient aggregation produces weighted average by dataset size, Gaussian noise with given epsilon/delta is applied, gradient norm outliers are rejected. mTLS integration test simulates valid and revoked client certificate connections and asserts correct accept/reject behavior. Partner client container must be tested in an isolated Docker network to confirm it cannot reach any aggregator infrastructure other than gRPC port 50051. Opacus DP noise parameters (epsilon, delta, max_grad_norm) must be logged to federation_rounds and never hardcoded in model training code; injected via config file. All gradient exchange serialization uses NumPy float32 to bound transmission size.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Gaussian DP noise at epsilon=1.0 significantly degrades GCN model accuracy on small hERG partner datasets (500-2,000 compounds); mitigated by empirical epsilon sweep in Phase 2 and accepting higher epsilon if accuracy loss exceeds 5% AUC-ROC. Risk: Non-IID compound distributions (e.g., one partner has only congeneric series) cause FedAvg to produce biased global model; mitigated by monitoring per-partner local AUC after applying global model and switching to FedProx if heterogeneity is severe. Risk: ACI restart re-establishes Key Vault connection; Key Vault throttling could delay round start; mitigated by retry logic with exponential backoff. Tradeoff: FedAvg over FedProx sacrifices convergence on heterogeneous data for implementation simplicity; appropriate for prototype evaluation phase. Risk: partner certificate compromise enables adversarial gradient submission; mitigated by gradient norm anomaly detection and revocation procedure.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Flower over PySyft for cleaner gRPC abstractions and active maintenance. Chose PyTorch GCN over fingerprint-based MLP because molecular graph representation captures structural features relevant to hERG channel binding without requiring predefined fingerprint bit strings. Confirmed FedAvg as starting aggregation strategy; FedProx deferred to Phase 2 based on non-IID analysis. Accepted epsilon=1.0 as initial DP budget; sensitivity analysis scheduled for Phase 2. mTLS chosen over API keys for partner authentication to avoid shared-secret management across companies.

metadata.notes: Cross-pharma federated learning toxicity prototype with mTLS, differential privacy, and GDPR compliance for partner_facing gradient sharing.

project_goal.value: Regulatory submission preparation portal that helps regulatory affairs teams assemble, validate, and track eCTD-compliant Module 2 and Module 5 dossiers for IND and NDA filings with the FDA and EMA
project_goal.rationale: Manual eCTD assembly is error-prone and time-consuming; automated validation and workflow tracking reduces submission rejection risk

target_users.value: Regulatory affairs writers, submission coordinators, and subject matter experts (clinical, CMC, nonclinical)
target_users.rationale: Cross-functional team collaborates on dossier assembly; each role has distinct permissions and task types

project_class.rationale: Browser-based collaboration platform with multi-user concurrent dossier editing and workflow management

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, admin_panel, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack collaboration platform with FDA/EMA gateway integration, document validation batch jobs, and submission timeline analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Large document volumes (hundreds of clinical study reports), complex eCTD structural validation, concurrent multi-user editing, and regulatory agency API integrations

risk_level.rationale: NDA dossiers contain pre-competitive drug data; submission failures or data leaks have major regulatory and IP consequences

data_sensitivity.rationale: Clinical study reports, CMC data, and nonclinical safety data in NDA dossiers are highly proprietary pre-market drug information

external_exposure.rationale: All access requires Okta OIDC authentication; no public-facing endpoints except the Okta redirect

access_model.value: RBAC with roles: writer (upload documents, edit dossier sections), coordinator (manage timelines, assign tasks, submit to agency), reviewer (approve sections), admin (manage submissions, users, validation rules)
access_model.rationale: Regulatory submission roles map directly to GxP function-based access control requirements

feature_scope.value: eCTD Module 2/5 dossier builder, document upload with eCTD naming convention validator, structural completeness checker, agency submission timeline tracker, FDA/EMA query response management, SME task assignment with deadline notifications, submission analytics dashboard, admin configuration for validation rules
feature_scope.rationale: Covers the full regulatory submission preparation and agency interaction lifecycle

mvp_scope.value: eCTD document upload with naming validator, Module 2/5 structural completeness checker, submission timeline tracker, multi-user task assignment, Okta OIDC RBAC
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP delivers core document management and validation; agency gateway integration in Phase 2

security_baseline.value: Okta OIDC with corporate SSO, RBAC, AES-256 at rest for documents, TLS 1.3, document access audit log, version control for all dossier changes
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential drug data requires strong access controls and full auditability for 21 CFR Part 11 compliance

frontend_stack.rationale: React component ecosystem supports complex document tree rendering; Vite for fast development builds

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Django 5
backend_stack.rationale: Django ORM and admin for rapid RBAC and document model development; Django REST Framework for API layer

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for relational data; S3 for document storage with versioning
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for dossier metadata and workflow state; S3 versioned bucket for document binary storage with encryption

hosting_target.value: ECS Fargate on AWS us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed container hosting with auto-scaling; us-east-1 for low latency to FDA ESG gateway (US-based)

privacy_retention_policy.value: Approved NDA dossiers retained permanently; working drafts and superseded versions retained 10 years; audit logs retained 15 years per 21 CFR Part 11
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FDA regulations require retention of all records supporting approved drug applications

future_scope.value: FDA ESG gateway direct submission integration, EMA eSubmission portal integration, AI-assisted gap analysis for missing dossier sections, cross-submission document reuse library
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 agency integrations eliminate manual file transfer; AI gap analysis reduces writer workload

constraints.value: Must deploy to AWS us-east-1; documents must never leave AWS us-east-1 boundary; budget under $5,000/month; system must support 200 concurrent users during submission crunch periods
constraints.rationale: Data residency for US drug data; cost budget for Phase 1; concurrency for parallel submission team activity during FDA filing deadlines

observability_baseline.value: Splunk log aggregation; custom metrics for document validation error rates, submission timeline adherence, and agency query response SLAs
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate Splunk standard; domain-specific metrics needed for regulatory operations KPIs

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprint cycles with regulatory stakeholder demos; GitHub Actions CI with 85% coverage gate
execution_preference.rationale: Short sprint cycles keep regulatory team engaged; coverage gate ensures document validation logic is tested

compliance_context.value: FDA 21 CFR Part 312 (IND), 21 CFR Part 314 (NDA), EMA eCTD guidelines, ICH M8 eCTD technical specification; 21 CFR Part 11 for electronic records and signatures
compliance_context.rationale: Platform that creates, modifies, and archives regulatory submissions must comply with electronic records regulations and agency eCTD technical standards

project.goal: eCTD-compliant dossier assembly, validation, and agency submission tracking platform
project.target_users: Regulatory writers, coordinators, SMEs, and reviewers

frontend.state: React Query for document tree and task data

security.auth: Okta OIDC + corporate SSO

open_questions.esg_gateway: FDA ESG gateway API credentials and test environment access not yet provisioned for Phase 2 integration

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 2 must address the two audit findings: incomplete eCTD naming convention validation coverage and missing 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature controls for dossier section approval. The naming convention validator covers Section 2.5 summary documents but was found to miss Module 5 clinical study report file name patterns. Both gaps would cause submission rejection at agency receipt.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Django's built-in admin is appropriate for the admin_panel capability. The S3 presigned URL approach for document download is correct for large file handling without proxying through the API. The remaining architectural gap from Round 1 is that document version history queries on S3 versioned bucket are slow for dossiers with hundreds of documents; a document_versions table in PostgreSQL caching S3 version metadata would reduce latency.
reasoner_reviews.security: ISS-001 (missing 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signatures) is the critical unresolved issue. Without cryptographic-quality electronic signatures on dossier section approvals, the platform cannot claim 21 CFR Part 11 compliance. AWS KMS asymmetric signing (RSA-3072) applied to the approval record hash is the recommended implementation.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 200 concurrent user target is achievable with ECS Fargate auto-scaling (4-12 tasks, CPU 60% target). PostgreSQL RDS Multi-AZ handles submission crunch periods. Redis 7 ElastiCache for session caching reduces DB load during peak concurrent access. Budget of $5,000/month is tight but achievable with reserved Fargate baseline.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Two rounds and the electronic signature gap remains unresolved. ISS-001 is a regulatory compliance blocker; the platform cannot be deployed to production regulatory teams without it. ISS-002 (incomplete eCTD filename validator) would cause silent submission package errors. Both must be fully resolved in Round 2.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with React Router v6 and route-based code splitting. Main sections: DossierWorkspace (module tree, section status, upload), SubmissionTimeline (Gantt-style timeline, milestone tracking), QueryManager (agency query inbox, response drafting), AdminConsole (validation rule config, user management).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dossier Builder: hierarchical eCTD module tree (M2.5, M2.7, M5.3) with document slots showing status (missing/uploaded/approved); document upload with inline filename validation; section approval button (triggers electronic signature flow). Agency Query Inbox: incoming FDA/EMA query display with response thread, SME assignment picker, deadline countdown. Submission Timeline: Gantt chart with milestone dates, task completion percentage, critical path highlighting.
frontend.state_management: React Query for dossier document tree (cache invalidated on upload/approval), submission timeline milestones, and agency query list. Zustand for upload modal state, selected dossier section, active filter panel.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: Okta SDK with PKCE; JWT stored in HttpOnly cookie via Django session; document download links are S3 presigned URLs with 5-minute expiry; no document binary content served from React bundle

backend.service_design: Django 5 application with REST Framework API. Apps: submissions (dossier, document, section models), workflows (task, timeline, milestone models), signatures (electronic signature records), agency_comms (query, response models). Celery workers for batch eCTD validation and S3 version sync jobs.
backend.api_patterns: REST with DRF serializers and OpenAPI 3.0 schema at /api/v1. Bulk endpoints for document batch upload status. Presigned URL endpoint for secure S3 download. Pagination on document list and audit log endpoints.
backend.background_jobs: Celery beat: nightly eCTD structural completeness check job scanning all open submissions for missing required sections; weekly S3 version metadata sync to document_versions PostgreSQL table. Celery worker: async document upload validation (filename convention, file type, virus scan via ClamAV) triggered on S3 upload event via SQS.
backend.failure_handling: Document upload validation failure returns structured error list (field: ectd_filename_error, rule: M5_CSR_filename_pattern) to frontend. Celery task failures logged to Splunk with submission_id and document_id for ops triage.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC with corporate SSO; Django social-auth-app-django for token exchange; session stored in Redis 7 ElastiCache; 30-minute idle timeout; re-authentication required for electronic signature action
security.authorization_model: Django custom permission classes per DRF viewset: writer (create/upload), coordinator (create submissions, assign tasks, initiate submission), reviewer (read-only section view + electronic signature approval), admin (all operations including validation rule management)
security.audit_and_logging_controls: document_access_log table: user_id, document_id, action ENUM view/download/upload/approve, timestamp, ip_address, session_id. Shipped to Splunk via Fluentd. 21 CFR Part 11: electronic signature records (user_id, action, reason_text, signed_at, kms_signature_hex, record_hash_sha256) stored in signatures table; immutable after creation.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for Django SECRET_KEY, PostgreSQL DSN, Okta client secret; 30-day rotation. S3 bucket encrypted with SSE-KMS using customer-managed key. KMS asymmetric key (RSA-3072) for electronic signatures.

data.entities: submissions (id, drug_name, regulatory_pathway ENUM IND/NDA/MAA, target_agency ENUM FDA/EMA, submission_type ENUM original/amendment, status, created_by, created_at), dossier_sections (id, submission_id FK, module ENUM M2_5/M2_7/M5_3, section_code VARCHAR e.g. 5.3.1, title, status ENUM empty/draft/uploaded/approved, approved_by, approved_at), documents (id, section_id FK, filename VARCHAR, s3_key, s3_version_id, file_size_bytes, ectd_filename_valid BOOLEAN, upload_by, upload_at), signatures (id, user_id, document_id FK, action VARCHAR, reason_text TEXT, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, kms_signature_hex TEXT, record_hash_sha256 VARCHAR), agency_queries (id, submission_id FK, agency ENUM FDA/EMA, query_reference VARCHAR, received_at, due_date, status ENUM open/in_progress/responded, assigned_to), submission_timelines (id, submission_id FK, milestone_name VARCHAR, planned_date DATE, actual_date DATE, status ENUM pending/completed/overdue)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for all metadata. S3 versioned bucket with SSE-KMS for document binaries. Document_versions PostgreSQL table caching S3 version metadata to avoid S3 list_object_versions latency on large dossiers.
data.retention_and_deletion: Approved NDA documents: S3 Intelligent-Tiering, no deletion policy (permanent). Draft documents: S3 Standard for 12 months then Glacier for 9 years then delete. Audit logs: PostgreSQL table for 1 year, then exported to S3 Glacier for 14 additional years (15-year total per 21 CFR Part 11).

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: web-service (Django + Gunicorn, 4-12 tasks with CPU 60% auto-scaling target), celery-worker (2-8 tasks with SQS queue depth scaling), celery-beat (1 task, no scaling). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c. ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ. S3 bucket us-east-1. ALB with WAF. CloudFront for React SPA static assets.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest 85% coverage gate, mypy strict, ruff, Django check --deploy, Docker build and push ECR, ECS rolling deployment via ecs-deploy. DB migrations via Django migrate in ECS one-off task before deployment. Separate staging environment with production-parity data snapshot for regression testing before production deploy.
devops.observability_stack: Splunk log aggregation via Fluentd sidecar on all Fargate tasks. CloudWatch container metrics (CPU, memory, request count). Custom metrics: ectd_validation_errors_per_submission, agency_query_response_days_overdue, submission_milestone_adherence_pct, document_upload_throughput. PagerDuty alerts on API error rate >2%, Celery worker queue depth >100, RDS CPU >70%.

ISS-001.title: Missing 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature controls for section approvals
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 section approval workflow recorded user_id and timestamp in dossier_sections but did not implement cryptographic electronic signatures as required by 21 CFR Part 11 Section 11.70; platform cannot be used for regulated submissions without this control

history.note: Identified by security and compliance review during Round 1 audit

ISS-002.title: eCTD filename validator missing Module 5 CSR naming patterns
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 eCTD filename validator covered Module 2.5 and 2.7 naming conventions but did not implement ICH M8 Module 5 clinical study report filename patterns (e.g., m5-3-1-{study_id}-{doc_type}.pdf); invalid filenames pass validation silently causing submission package errors

history.note: Identified during QA testing with a real NDA submission package

focus_issues.title: Missing 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature controls for section approvals
focus_issues.detail: Must implement cryptographic electronic signatures (AWS KMS RSA-3072 asymmetric signing of record hash) for all dossier section approval actions

focus_issues.title: eCTD filename validator missing Module 5 CSR naming patterns
focus_issues.detail: ICH M8 Module 5 filename regex patterns must be added to validation rule set; silent pass on invalid names must be replaced with structured validation errors

latest_recommendations: Implement ElectronicSignatureService using AWS KMS sign() with RSA-3072 asymmetric key; sign SHA256 hash of (user_id + document_id + action + signed_at ISO string); store kms_signature_hex and record_hash_sha256 in signatures table; require re-authentication via Okta before signature action

Expand eCTDFilenameValidator to include all ICH M8 Module 5 section patterns; pattern library should be loaded from database table (validation_rules) managed by admin UI so regulatory ops can update without code deployment

convergence_state.reason: Critical ISS-001 and high ISS-002 unresolved; Round 2 must deliver both fixes before production deployment approval

previous_audits.summary: Good overall architecture for the regulatory domain but two compliance-critical gaps block production deployment: missing 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signatures and incomplete eCTD filename validation coverage for Module 5.
strengths: Django 5 + DRF is well-suited for the RBAC and document model complexity
S3 presigned URL pattern correctly avoids proxying large clinical study reports through the API
Celery async validation pipeline is the right pattern for document processing
Okta OIDC with RBAC is properly specified for all four user roles
concerns: ISS-001: section approval records only store user_id and timestamp; no cryptographic signature; fails 21 CFR Part 11 Section 11.70 electronic signature requirements
ISS-002: eCTD validator covers M2.5/M2.7 patterns only; M5 CSR names silently pass validation
blocking_issues: ISS-001 is a regulatory compliance blocker; no production deployment to regulated submission teams is possible without 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signatures
recommendations: Implement ElectronicSignatureService with AWS KMS asymmetric RSA-3072 signing of approval record hash; require Okta re-authentication before every signature action; store kms_signature_hex in immutable signatures table
Load eCTD validation patterns from validation_rules table managed by admin UI; add complete ICH M8 Module 5 pattern set (m5-3-1, m5-3-2, m5-3-3, m5-3-4, m5-3-5) with section-specific regex

issue_updates.title: Missing 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature controls
issue_updates.detail: Section approval records lack cryptographic signature; approval history is not non-repudiation-proof under 21 CFR Part 11

issue_updates.title: eCTD M5 filename validator gap
issue_updates.detail: Module 5 CSR filenames not validated; m5-3-1-STUDY001-fullcsr.pdf passes validation when it should be caught as non-compliant by ICH M8 pattern check

previous_plan.title: eCTD Regulatory Submission Portal - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Django 5 REST API with React 18 frontend on ECS Fargate for eCTD dossier assembly, document upload with naming validation, submission timeline tracking, and multi-user RBAC via Okta OIDC. S3 versioned bucket for document storage.
previous_plan.system_components: ECS Fargate web-service and celery-worker, RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ, ElastiCache Redis, S3 versioned bucket SSE-KMS, ALB with WAF. Okta OIDC RBAC. Splunk log aggregation.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC, RBAC four roles, AES-256 S3 SSE-KMS, TLS 1.3, document_access_log table. Missing: 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signatures (ISS-001). eCTD validator incomplete for M5 (ISS-002).
previous_plan.data_model: submissions, dossier_sections (status includes approved_by user_id only), documents (ectd_filename_valid), agency_queries, submission_timelines. Signatures table absent in Round 1.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve the critical ISS-001 (21 CFR Part 11 electronic signatures) and high ISS-002 (incomplete eCTD M5 filename validation). ISS-001 fix requires implementing ElectronicSignatureService using AWS KMS asymmetric RSA-3072 signing plus Okta re-authentication gate, and adding the signatures table. ISS-002 fix requires loading eCTD validation patterns from a validation_rules table with full ICH M8 Module 5 pattern coverage, managed via admin UI. Both fixes are surgical additions to the otherwise solid Round 1 architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented ElectronicSignatureService: constructs record_hash = SHA256(f'{user_id}|{document_id}|{action}|{signed_at_iso}'), calls boto3 kms.sign(KeyId=RSA_3072_KEY_ARN, Message=record_hash, MessageType='DIGEST', SigningAlgorithm='RSASSA_PKCS1_V1_5_SHA_256'), stores kms_signature_hex and record_hash_sha256 in immutable signatures table. Okta re-authentication required: approval action redirects through Okta with prompt=login and max_age=0, blocking cached SSO sessions. signatures table rows are append-only (no UPDATE/DELETE permissions granted to application DB role). Okta acr_values=urn:okta:loa:2fa:any enforced for signature actions.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All dossier section approvals produce a cryptographically signed record verifiable against the KMS public key; re-authentication prevents session hijacking attacks on signature actions; signatures table is non-repudiation-proof and meets 21 CFR Part 11 Section 11.70 requirements

fix_report.action_taken: Refactored ECTDFilenameValidator to load pattern rules from validation_rules PostgreSQL table managed via Django admin. Added complete ICH M8 Module 5 pattern set: M5.3.1 (m5-3-1-{study_id}-{doc_type}.pdf), M5.3.2 (m5-3-2-{study_id}-{doc_type}.pdf), M5.3.3 (m5-3-3-{study_id}-{doc_type}.pdf), M5.3.4 (m5-3-4-{study_id}-{doc_type}.pdf), M5.3.5 (m5-3-5-{study_id}-{doc_type}.pdf). Validator returns structured ValidationError list with fields: filename, section_code, violated_rule, expected_pattern. Invalid filenames blocked at upload API; frontend displays per-file error inline in the dossier section document slot.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All ICH M8 Module 5 filename patterns validated on upload; invalid filenames return structured errors before S3 storage; admin can add new patterns without code deployment; submission packages contain only eCTD-compliant document names

target_output.title: eCTD Regulatory Submission Preparation Portal
target_output.executive_summary: A RBAC-controlled web platform built on Django 5 and React 18 that enables regulatory affairs teams to assemble eCTD-compliant Module 2 and Module 5 dossiers, validate ICH M8 document structures, track FDA and EMA submission timelines, and manage agency query responses. Round 2 adds 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signatures via AWS KMS RSA-3072 signing for section approvals (ISS-001) and extends the eCTD filename validator to cover all Module 5 clinical study report naming patterns via a database-driven validation rule set (ISS-002).
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture on AWS ECS Fargate: React 18 SPA served via CloudFront communicating with a Django 5 REST API behind ALB with WAF. API connects to RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ for dossier metadata and Redis 7 ElastiCache for sessions. Documents stored in S3 versioned bucket with SSE-KMS; large file downloads served via presigned URLs (5-minute expiry). Celery workers handle async document validation (filename convention, ClamAV virus scan) triggered by S3 event notifications via SQS. Electronic signatures use AWS KMS asymmetric RSA-3072 signing with Okta re-authentication gate. ECTDFilenameValidator loads pattern rules from PostgreSQL validation_rules table managed via Django admin, enabling pattern updates without code deployment.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 TypeScript, Vite 5, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, React Router v6. Backend: Python 3.12 Django 5, Django REST Framework 3.15, Celery 5, django-storages for S3. Auth: Okta OIDC via social-auth-app-django; Redis session backend; re-authentication via Okta prompt=login for signature actions. Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 Multi-AZ. Storage: S3 versioned bucket SSE-KMS. Security: AWS KMS RSA-3072 for electronic signatures, ClamAV in Celery worker for virus scanning. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB + WAF, CloudFront, GitHub Actions CI, Splunk via Fluentd.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Dossier Builder: hierarchical eCTD module tree (M2.5 clinical overview, M2.7 clinical summaries, M5.3 clinical study reports) with document slots showing status; document upload with ECTDFilenameValidator inline error display; section approval with Okta re-auth and KMS electronic signature. Agency Query Manager: FDA/EMA query inbox with response thread, SME assignment, deadline countdown, status tracking. Submission Timeline: milestone Gantt with planned vs actual dates, critical path highlighting, milestone completion percentage. Admin Console: validation rule management for eCTD patterns (add/edit M5 patterns without deployment), user RBAC management, submission configuration. Analytics Dashboard: submission milestone adherence rate, agency query response time distribution, open submission count by regulatory pathway.
target_output.system_components: ECS Fargate cluster: web-service (Django + Gunicorn, 4-12 tasks, CPU 60% auto-scaling), celery-worker (2-8 tasks, SQS queue depth HPA), celery-beat (1 task). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (submissions, dossier_sections, documents, signatures, validation_rules, agency_queries, document_access_log). ElastiCache Redis 7 Multi-AZ (sessions, Celery broker). S3 versioned bucket SSE-KMS (document binaries, audit log archives). ALB with WAF OWASP rule set. CloudFront for React SPA static assets. ElectronicSignatureService (KMS RSA-3072 asymmetric signing). ECTDFilenameValidator (loads patterns from validation_rules table). ClamAV Celery task (virus scan on upload). Fluentd sidecar (log shipping to Splunk). AWS Secrets Manager (Django SECRET_KEY, PostgreSQL DSN, Okta client secret).
target_output.workflows: Dossier Section Approval (ISS-001 fix): coordinator or reviewer navigates to dossier section, clicks Approve; frontend redirects to Okta with prompt=login and acr_values=urn:okta:loa:2fa:any; on successful re-authentication, frontend calls POST /api/v1/signatures with {document_id, action: 'approve', reason_text}; ElectronicSignatureService constructs record_hash = SHA256(user_id + document_id + 'approve' + signed_at_iso), calls KMS sign with RSA-3072 key, stores {kms_signature_hex, record_hash_sha256, user_id, document_id, action, reason_text, signed_at} in signatures table (append-only); dossier_sections.status updated to approved; document_access_log entry written. Document Upload with Filename Validation (ISS-002 fix): writer uploads PDF to POST /api/v1/documents/upload; ECTDFilenameValidator loads applicable patterns from validation_rules for the target section_code; validates filename against all loaded regex patterns; returns ValidationError list if any pattern fails (fields: filename, section_code, violated_rule, expected_pattern); on pass, Django signals S3 upload via presigned POST; SQS event triggers Celery virus scan task; on clean scan result, document record created and section status updated to uploaded.
target_output.data_model: submissions (id UUID PK, drug_name VARCHAR, regulatory_pathway ENUM ind/nda/maa, target_agency ENUM fda/ema, submission_type ENUM original/amendment, status ENUM draft/in_review/submitted/approved, created_by FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). dossier_sections (id UUID PK, submission_id FK, module ENUM m2_5/m2_7/m5_3, section_code VARCHAR e.g. 5.3.1, title VARCHAR, status ENUM empty/draft/uploaded/approved, approved_by FK nullable, approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable). documents (id UUID PK, section_id FK, filename VARCHAR, s3_key VARCHAR, s3_version_id VARCHAR, file_size_bytes BIGINT, ectd_filename_valid BOOLEAN, upload_by FK, upload_at TIMESTAMPTZ). signatures (id UUID PK, user_id FK, document_id FK, action VARCHAR, reason_text TEXT, signed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, kms_signature_hex TEXT, record_hash_sha256 VARCHAR, kms_key_id VARCHAR; NO UPDATE/DELETE privilege for app DB role). validation_rules (id UUID PK, section_code VARCHAR, module ENUM, pattern_regex TEXT, description TEXT, icm_reference VARCHAR, active BOOLEAN, created_by FK, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). agency_queries (id UUID PK, submission_id FK, agency ENUM, query_reference VARCHAR, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ, due_date DATE, status ENUM open/in_progress/responded, assigned_to FK). document_access_log (id UUID PK, user_id FK, document_id FK, action ENUM view/download/upload/approve, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, ip_address INET, session_id VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1 with OpenAPI 3.0. POST /documents/upload (multipart with section_id; returns validation errors or 201 with document metadata). GET /dossier-sections/:id (section tree with document slots and status). POST /signatures (electronic signature for approval; requires re-auth JWT with acr claim). GET /signatures/:document_id (signature verification response with kms_signature_hex and record_hash). GET /submissions/:id/timeline (milestone Gantt data). GET /agency-queries?submission_id=&status=open (query inbox). PATCH /agency-queries/:id (assign SME, update status). GET /admin/validation-rules (admin-only: list rules). POST /admin/validation-rules (admin-only: create new eCTD pattern). All endpoints: Okta JWT Bearer; RBAC permission class per viewset; rate limit 300 req/min per user via DRF throttle.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC with corporate SSO; session in Redis with 30-minute idle timeout. RBAC enforced per DRF viewset: POST /signatures requires reviewer or coordinator role with acr claim confirming re-authentication. 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signatures: AWS KMS RSA-3072 asymmetric signing of approval record hash; kms_signature_hex stored in append-only signatures table; verifiable via KMS verify() using public key. Okta re-authentication (prompt=login, acr_values=urn:okta:loa:2fa:any) required before every signature action. S3 SSE-KMS with customer-managed key for document encryption at rest. TLS 1.3 on ALB listeners. WAF OWASP rule set blocks SQL injection, XSS, oversized file uploads. ClamAV virus scan on all uploaded documents before document record creation. AWS Secrets Manager for all credentials with 30-day rotation. Audit log: document_access_log shipped to Splunk; signatures table is the non-repudiation record. Compliance: 21 CFR Part 11 Section 11.70 satisfied by KMS asymmetric signatures; ICH M8 eCTD technical specification enforced by validation_rules pattern set; audit trail retention 15 years per 21 CFR Part 11.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster us-east-1 with three services: web-service (Django + Gunicorn workers=4, 4-12 Fargate tasks, CPU 60% auto-scaling target, 1-minute scale-in cooldown), celery-worker (2-8 tasks, scales on SQS queue depth >50), celery-beat (1 task, no scaling). ALB target group health check /api/v1/health (no auth). CloudFront distribution for React SPA (S3 origin, /api/* forwarded to ALB). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c with read replica for Celery beat query load. ElastiCache Redis 7 with Multi-AZ failover. GitHub Actions CI: pytest 85% coverage gate (includes ElectronicSignatureService KMS mock tests and ECTDFilenameValidator Module 5 pattern tests), mypy strict, ruff, Django check --deploy, Docker build and push ECR, ecs-deploy rolling update. Django migrate in ECS one-off task pre-deployment. Staging environment with anonymized production dossier metadata for regression testing. Secrets Manager rotation for Django SECRET_KEY and PostgreSQL password every 30 days.
target_output.observability: Fluentd sidecar on all ECS tasks ships structured JSON logs to Splunk. CloudWatch container CPU and memory metrics. Custom Splunk dashboards: ectd_validation_errors_per_submission (by module and section_code), signature_actions_per_day, agency_query_response_days (distribution against due_date), submission_milestone_adherence_pct. PagerDuty alerts: API error rate >2% in 5-minute window, Celery queue depth >100 (validation backlog), RDS CPU >70%, ElastiCache memory >80%, ECS service task count drops below minimum. KMS API error rate tracked via CloudWatch KMS metrics; alert on >0 KMS signing failures (electronic signature service health).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $4,200/month within $5,000 budget: ECS Fargate web-service (4 tasks baseline reserved) ~$900, celery-worker (2 tasks baseline) ~$450, RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.large ~$1,200, ElastiCache r6g.large Multi-AZ ~$400, S3 standard storage 5TB NDA documents ~$115, CloudFront ~$50, ALB + WAF ~$200, KMS (10K signature operations/month) ~$15, Secrets Manager ~$30, Splunk Fargate data ingestion ~$700. Submission crunch auto-scaling to 12 web tasks and 8 celery tasks adds ~$500 during peak months.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (done - Round 1 baseline): Django RBAC, dossier builder UI, M2.5/M2.7 eCTD validator, document upload to S3, submission timeline tracker, Celery async validation, Okta OIDC. Phase 2 (current - Round 2): ISS-001 KMS electronic signatures with Okta re-auth, ISS-002 complete ICH M8 Module 5 validation patterns via validation_rules table, ClamAV virus scan Celery task, document_versions PostgreSQL cache for S3 version queries, production deployment readiness. Phase 3 (8 weeks): FDA ESG gateway direct eCTD submission integration, EMA eSubmission portal integration, PDF/A compliance validation for uploaded documents. Phase 4 (12 weeks): AI-assisted gap analysis for missing required dossier sections, cross-submission document reuse library, regulatory intelligence dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: ElectronicSignatureService unit tests must use Moto for KMS mock; test cases: successful signing returns valid kms_signature_hex, KMS signing failure raises SignatureServiceError (does not fall back to unsigned approval), re-authentication acr claim validation correctly rejects tokens without MFA acr. ECTDFilenameValidator tests must include fixtures for all ICH M8 Module 5 section patterns (5.3.1 through 5.3.5) covering valid and invalid examples. validation_rules patterns must include icm_reference field linking to the ICH M8 section for traceability. signatures table migrations must include DB-level constraint preventing UPDATE or DELETE; grant only INSERT and SELECT to application DB role. All new eCTD pattern additions by admin must be logged to document_access_log with action='validation_rule_created' for audit trail.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: KMS RSA-3072 signing latency (typically 50-100ms) adds overhead to section approval flow; mitigated by async feedback pattern (approval optimistic UI while signature completes, display confirmation on return from Okta re-auth). Risk: Okta re-authentication introduces friction for high-volume approval workflows during submission crunch; accepted tradeoff for 21 CFR Part 11 compliance; bulk approval with single Okta re-auth for up to 10 sections deferred to Phase 3. Risk: validation_rules table misconfiguration by admin could allow non-compliant filenames to pass; mitigated by requiring admin role for rule changes and logging all rule modifications. Risk: ClamAV virus database staleness on long-running Celery worker containers; mitigated by nightly container restart policy updating ClamAV signatures.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed AWS KMS asymmetric RSA-3072 over internal PKI for electronic signatures: KMS managed service eliminates certificate rotation complexity and provides CloudWatch auditability of all signing operations. Confirmed validation_rules database-driven approach over hardcoded regex constants: regulatory ops team requires ability to add new eCTD patterns (e.g., ICH M8 revision additions) without code deployment. Accepted ClamAV over commercial AV for virus scanning to avoid third-party vendor data access to clinical study report content. Confirmed S3 versioned bucket over document management system: S3 versioning provides immutable version history required by 21 CFR Part 11 without additional vendor software.

metadata.notes: eCTD regulatory submission portal Round 2: resolves 21 CFR Part 11 electronic signature gap (ISS-001) and Module 5 eCTD filename validator coverage (ISS-002).

project_goal.value: AI-assisted literature mining system that extracts drug-target interaction evidence from PubMed abstracts and full-text articles, structuring findings into a knowledge graph to support target validation decisions
project_goal.rationale: Core capability for competitive intelligence and target identification pipeline

target_users.value: Target validation scientists and computational biologists
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of extracted drug-target evidence

project_class.rationale: LLM-driven NER and relation extraction pipeline with knowledge graph output

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack with LLM extraction, graph storage, and scheduled ingestion jobs

complexity_level.rationale: 50M+ PubMed records, concurrent scientist queries, knowledge graph at scale

risk_level.rationale: Competitive intelligence data and proprietary target annotations require strict access control

data_sensitivity.rationale: Extracted competitive intelligence and internal target annotations are proprietary IP

external_exposure.rationale: Internal tool requiring Auth0 OIDC login for all access

access_model.value: RBAC with scientist, curator, and admin roles; read-only for scientists, annotation write for curators
access_model.rationale: Curation annotations are high-value; write access must be controlled

feature_scope.value: PubMed batch ingestion, LLM-based NER and relation extraction, Neo4j knowledge graph, evidence search UI, annotation and curation workflow, analytics dashboards for extraction quality
feature_scope.rationale: End-to-end pipeline from raw literature to curated evidence

mvp_scope.value: Daily PubMed abstract ingestion, GPT-4o extraction pipeline, Neo4j graph with drug-target edges, basic search UI with evidence citations
mvp_scope.rationale: Core extraction and browse capability is the primary value
